articles.title: Privacy, accountability and overreach: A detailed look at the Karnataka HC’s judgment on Proton mail
articles.summary: Privacy, accountability and overreach: A detailed look at the Karnataka HC’s judgment on Proton mail.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr On April 29, 2025, the Karnataka High Court delivered a judgment directing the blocking of Proton Mail in India. The case stemmed from a petition filed by a Bengaluru-based company, whose employees had reportedly faced targeted abuse via anonymous Proton Mail accounts. Although the judgment was reserved on March 21, 2025, it was not made public until May 29, 2025, when it was uploaded to the High Court’s website. In the meanwhile, we relied on courtroom observations and media reports to interpret the outcome. As part of our ongoing efforts to monitor and analyze blocking orders issued under Section 69A of the IT Act, 2000 we have now reviewed the full judgment and offer this detailed analysis. Why should you care? This case may seem like a specific dispute between a private company and an email service, but its implications are far-reaching. Proton Mail and similar encrypted platforms are not just used by criminals or miscreants, they are crucial tools for journalists, whistleblowers, activists, and ordinary citizens who want to protect their communications from surveillance, data mining, and government overreach. By entertaining the idea of banning Proton Mail or similar platforms due to their strong privacy features, courts risk setting a dangerous precedent. If this judgment is followed by executive actions or similar judicial orders in the future, we could see more privacy-focused platforms being restricted or banned in India. Background On April 29, 2025, the Karnataka High Court delivered its judgment in response to a Writ Petition No. 2358 of 2025 filed by M. Moser Design Associates (India) Pvt. Ltd. , an architecture and interior design Company having pan India presence including Bengaluru. The company approached the Court after multiple employees, including senior female personnel, were targeted with a series of disturbing and obscene emails sent from anonymous Proton Mail addresses. These messages contained obscene language, sexually explicit morphed images, and defamatory content, and were also shared with clients, vendors, and competitors which caused emotional trauma and reputational harm to the female employees of the firm. The emails originated from Proton Mail, a privacy-focused email service headquartered in Switzerland, known for offering end-to-end encrypted communications . Although the company promptly disabled the reported accounts upon receiving complaints, it refused to disclose any information about the senders. Proton Mail cited its internal privacy policies and Swiss legal obligations, which mandate that any data requests be routed through formal law enforcement channels under mutual legal assistance treaties (“ MLAT” ). Despite the registration of an FIR and multiple efforts by the police to trace the origin of the emails, including issuing notices via INTERPOL, Proton Mail did not disclose the users' identities. As a result, the petitioner approached the Court seeking directions to the Government of India to invoke the blocking powers under Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, read with Rule 10 of the IT (Blocking Rules), 2009 . The Petitioner also contended that Proton Mail’s refusal to reveal the information of senders is a threat not just to individual safety but also to national security. The Court ultimately agreed with the petitioner’s concerns and directed the Central Government to initiate blocking of Proton Mail under the aforementioned legal provisions. It found that Proton Mail’s design that offers anonymous, encrypted communication with no physical or infrastructural presence in India, rendered it largely unaccountable under Indian law. While ruling this, the Court cited several past instances from both domestic and international incidents where Proton Mail’s strong encryption and anonymity features allegedly facilitated serious offences. The court noted that in Karnataka last year, Proton Mail was reportedly used to send bomb threats to several government officials, including the Chief Minister. Similarly, in Tamil Nadu, a series of fake bomb threats traced back to Proton Mail accounts led the state to recommend a complete ban on the service, citing an inability to identify the perpetrators. Internationally, the Court referred to a 2022 case where fraudsters impersonated Indian embassy officials using Proton Mail addresses to scam travelers bound for the UAE. Beyond India, the judgment also referenced Russia’s decision to block Proton Mail, attributing it to national security concerns and the platform’s refusal to cooperate with law enforcement requests. Importantly, the Court also cited precedents including the Delhi High Court’s judgment in X v. Union of India (2021) , and referenced actions taken by the Indian government and foreign governments in similar circumstances in justification of the blocking order. However, despite this order, Proton Mail is still accessible for users in most parts of India. Blocking powers and procedure under the IT Act, 2000 and Rules thereunder The central legislative tool for blocking is Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, (“ IT Act, 2000” ) which authorizes the Central Government to block public access to any information in the interest of Sovereignty and integrity of India, Defence of India, Security of the State, Friendly relations with foreign states, Public order, or Prevention of incitement to any cognizable offence. To regulate this power, the government enacted the Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards for Blocking for Access of Information by Public) Rules, 2009 (“Blocking Rules, 2009” ) Under these regulations, blocking of online content is permissible through two routes: first, by an order issued by the Designated Officer under Section 69A of the IT Act, 2000 in strict compliance with the 2009 Blocking Rules; or second, pursuant to a judicial order passed by a court of competent jurisdiction and only where the content in question relates to unlawful acts that fall squarely within the ambit of Article 19(2). For executive blocking, as per Rules 4 to 8, the process typically begins with a complaint submitted to a designated ‘Nodal Officer’ within a government ministry or department. This officer must scrutinize the request to determine whether it satisfies the criteria laid down under Section 69A of the IT Act, 2000. If it does, the request is forwarded to the ‘Designated Officer’, currently the Group Coordinator, Cyberlaw Division, Ministry of Communications and Information Technology, who then refers it to a committee for review as provided in Rule 8. This committee is expected to give the intermediary or person hosting the impugned content an opportunity to be heard. Only after the committee’s recommendation is approved by the Secretary, Department of Information Technology, can access to the content be blocked (Rule 8(4)). Even in urgent situations under Rule 9, where interim blocking may be ordered without prior committee review, such an order must be later confirmed through the same safeguards. Whereas, the procedure for implementing court-directed blocking is governed by Rule 10 of the Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards for Blocking for Access of Information by Public) Rules, 2009 . Under this provision, when a competent Indian court issues a blocking order, a certified copy of the order is submitted to the Designated Officer, who is required to immediately forward it to the Secretary, Department of Information Technology and to initiate action as directed by the court. This process is distinct from executive-initiated blocking under Rules 6 to 9, which involves an inter-ministerial committee, notice to the originator or intermediary, and a reasoned recommendation. In contrast, court-directed blocking bypasses the internal committee review since the direction comes from a judicial authority and the Designated Officer’s role is limited to executing the court’s specific instructions. Importantly, Rule 16 requires that all information relating to such blocking requests and actions remain confidential, further limiting public transparency and accountability. This mechanism ensures swift enforcement of judicial orders but also raises concerns about the lack of procedural safeguards for content creators or affected intermediaries, particularly when ex parte interim injunctions lead to overbroad or indefinite content restrictions. Key takeaways: Legal framework and court's analysis This judgment is a comprehensive study of India’s cyber law architecture, data privacy jurisprudence, and the challenges of cross-border digital crime. In deciding whether to direct a ban on Proton Mail or merely urge procedural compliance, the Court did not just adjudicate a writ petition, it undertook the more delicate task of balancing technological liberty with digital accountability. Excerpt from the judgment in Writ Petition No. 2358 of 2025 The Court reiterated that blocking is an extreme measure requiring designated‑officer and committee scrutiny, yet insists courts cannot stay inert when online abuse, especially of women, undermines privacy and integrity. Excerpt from the judgment in Writ Petition No. 2358 of 2025 In this context, it examined the IT (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules, 2011 and Section 79 of the Information Technology Act, 2000, which offers conditional immunity to intermediaries from liability for third-party content. This immunity is lost if intermediaries conspire in unlawful acts or fail to remove access to such content upon receiving government or court notice. The Court held that intermediaries like Proton Mail are obligated to disable access to abusive content when notified and that failure to act, particularly after gaining actual knowledge, may attract liability under Indian law—even if the entity is based abroad. While Proton Mail had complied partially by disabling the offending accounts, it refused to disclose sender information or assist Indian authorities, citing Swiss legal obligations. Jurisdiction over foreign entities Then, the Court turned to Section 75 of the IT Act, which extends the statute’s reach to offences committed abroad when the affected computer system or network is located in India. Because the abusive emails were received and caused harm here, both the senders and Proton Mail fell within the Act’s ambit. Yet the Court conceded that legal jurisdiction does not ensure practical enforceability: headquartered in Switzerland with no servers or infrastructure in India, Proton Mail remains bound by Swiss law, making compliance with Indian orders difficult despite the formal reach of Section 75. International legal cooperation: MLAT and BNSS The Court also scrutinised the 1989 India–Switzerland Mutual Legal Assistance Treaty (MLAT) , which enables cross‑border evidence sharing and service of documents in criminal matters. Both the petitioners and the bench acknowledged, however, that the MLAT process is slow, procedurally complex, and ill‑suited to fast‑moving cybercrime investigations. To bolster international cooperation, the Court pointed to Section 112 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023, which empowers Indian courts to issue formal requests for evidence to foreign authorities. Yet, even after such requests were transmitted to Switzerland, the sought‑after user information remained unavailable. Privacy vs. Public Safety: Proton Mail’s Policy in the Dock The Court then turned to Proton Mail’s own privacy policy, noting that (i) the service is governed exclusively by Swiss law; (ii) it will not respond to foreign data requests unless channelled through Swiss authorities; (iii) its end‑to‑end encryption design prevents it from decrypting user content; and (iv) even metadata is disclosed only in narrowly defined circumstances after judicial oversight in Switzerland. While the bench acknowledged that this privacy‑first architecture provides vital protection for journalists, activists, and other vulnerable users, it also warned that the same safeguards can become a refuge for malicious actors, creating a zone of near‑total impunity when international cooperation mechanisms break down. Judicial precedent from Delhi High Court The Court drew heavily from the Delhi High Court’s decision in X v. Union of India (2021), which dealt with online harassment and intermediary accountability. That judgment held that platforms must remove content within 24 hours of a court order, de-index offending URLs, and preserve metadata for 180 days to support investigations, all while prioritizing the right to privacy and dignity. Relying on this reasoning, the Karnataka High Court held that intermediaries cannot remain passive when serious harm is apparent—failure to act after notification can attract legal consequences. Why This Judgment Sets a Concerning Precedent and What Alternatives the Court Could Have Considered The Court’s decision of an outright ban on Proton Mail, signals a worrying shift in the legal landscape surrounding privacy and encryption in India. The blocking process under Section 69A is already shrouded in opacity. Despite the Supreme Court’s ruling in Shreya Singhal vs U.O.I orders remain confidential, hearings are one-sided, and users receive no meaningful notice or chance to contest the action. The Internet Freedom Foundation has represented several users in such cases, and experience shows how insubstantial these procedural safeguards are in practice. Penalizing this architecture could lead to a domino effect where encrypted platforms like Signal, Tutanota, or Matrix may now face mounting pressure to dilute their security features or risk being blocked. It will have a chilling effect on freedom of speech for journalists, activists, and vulnerable communities who rely on encryption for safety and anonymity. There is also the issue of legal uncertainty for intermediaries. Service providers operating from jurisdictions with strong data protection laws, such as Switzerland, could find themselves caught between complying with their home country’s privacy standards and adhering to Indian court directives. This legal limbo may ultimately deter such platforms from offering services in India altogether. Finally, the broader constitutional consequences cannot be ignored. By contemplating the blocking of secure platforms, the judgment risks diluting fundamental rights enshrined in the Indian Constitution, notably, the right to privacy as articulated in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India , and the right to freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a). Excerpt from the judgment in Writ Petition No. 2358 of 2025 In this scenario, instead of outright ban on Proton mail, a more balanced and constructive approach could have been considered. One key alternative would have been to underscore the urgent need for reforming the MLAT framework. The current MLAT process is notoriously slow and ill-suited for cybercrime investigations. By advocating for more efficient cross-border data-sharing mechanisms, the Court could have addressed law enforcement concerns without resorting to punitive measures against privacy tools. The Court could also have directed the government to establish clear, transparent protocols for addressing abuse complaints involving foreign intermediaries. Diplomatic channels could also have been explored. The 1989 bilateral treaty between India and Switzerland was drafted in a pre-digital era and remains outdated for addressing modern cybercrimes. Encouraging the government to renegotiate and update this treaty would be a step toward creating a robust legal infrastructure that respects both privacy and law enforcement needs. Ultimately, the judgment reflects a growing judicial unease with technologies that prioritize encryption and anonymity. However, this discomfort must not morph into overbroad policy that undermines fundamental rights. What India needs is smarter regulation, approaches that can effectively punish criminal misuse while preserving the digital architecture that protects free speech, dissent, and privacy. Important Documents Copy of judgment in M. Moser Design Associates (India) Pvt. Ltd. vs. Union of India & Ors. WP. (C) No. 2358 of 2025 [ Link ] Copy of judgment in X vs Union of India & Ors. W.P.(Crl) 1082/2020 & Crl. M.A. Nos.9485/2020, 10986-87/2020 [ Link ]

articles.title: Your Internet, Their Rules: Airtel's AI-driven internet filter poses censorship risks
articles.summary: Framed as fraud prevention, Airtel’s AI-based blocking system enables unchecked censorship, threatening user autonomy, privacy, and an open internet.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr Airtel’s new AI-based website blocking system designed to detect and block “fraudulent” websites goes beyond the licensing conditions under which ISPs operate in India. It raises serious concerns about net neutrality, privacy, and unchecked censorship. Why should you care? Because this affects your internet . Airtel is deciding, using AI, which websites you can and cannot visit without asking, informing, or allowing you to opt out. While the goal is to block scams, this may violate your digital rights and open the door to overreach, censorship, and invasive surveillance — all without public oversight. Background Amid the surge in digital fraud cases in India, on May 15, 2025, Airtel announced the launch of an AI-based security system designed to detect and block “fraudulent” websites. Screenshot of Bharti Airtel’s official press release dated May 15, 2025, retrieved from the company’s website on May 21, 2025 As per the press release this security service will cover all communications and over-the-top (“OTT”) platforms, emails, web browsers and SMS. It is claimed that the AI-powered security platform uses a multi-layered system to scan internet traffic in real time, using global threat databases and its own threat actor repository to block malicious websites across devices and platforms. It assigns a spam score to websites using internal signals and threat intelligence from sources such as global repositories and banks; high scores lead to the site being blocked before it loads. When a user attempts to access a flagged malicious site, the page is blocked and replaced with a notice explaining the reason. According to Airtel's MD Gopal Vittal, the tool has shown high accuracy in six months of trials and aims to make Airtel's networks fully secure from scams and spam. Screenshot of a pictorial representation from Bharti Airtel’s official press release dated May 15, 2025, retrieved from the company’s website on May 21, 2025 The new AI-powered blocking system, while aimed at security, could similarly undermine net neutrality principles by giving Airtel significant control over what content users can access potentially without transparent oversight or user consent. Moreover, India’s ISP market is highly oligopolistic, dominated by a few major players including Airtel, Jio, and Vodafone Idea, which amplifies the risks of such centralised control. When only a handful of companies manage the majority of internet traffic, policies like Airtel’s AI blocking platform could lead to inconsistent, arbitrary, or excessive content filtering, further limiting consumer choice and the free flow of information online. What does the law say? Development of Net Neutrality regulation in India The debate around net neutrality in India began gaining public attention in 2014, through one of the country’s most significant digital rights movements – SavetheInternet.in – which brought the issue into mainstream public discourse. In response, the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (“TRAI”) issued a consultation paper in 2015 on differential pricing for data services. Subsequently, TRAI released the “Prohibition of Discriminatory Tariffs for Data Services Regulations” in February 2016 . This regulation banned service providers from offering or charging different rates for data based on content, effectively disallowing zero-rating plans and paid prioritisation models. This was a landmark move, placing India among the early adopters of formal net neutrality protections. Following this, TRAI initiated a broader consultation on net neutrality in 2017, aimed at establishing a more comprehensive regulatory framework. Based on stakeholder feedback, TRAI released its final recommendations on November 28, 2017, advocating for strong net neutrality protections, including a ban on blocking, throttling, and paid prioritisation of content through amendments in the licensing terms for ISPs. These recommendations were formally accepted by the Department of Telecommunications (“DoT”) on July 31, 2018, as the “Regulatory Framework on Net Neutrality”, making it legally binding . The Regulatory Framework amended Unified Licenses for ISPs to incorporate net neutrality principles. However, DoT made some modifications, reserving for itself the role of defining Traffic Management Practices (“TMP”s), setting up a Multi-Stakeholder Body (“MSB”) for advisory purposes, and retaining monitoring and enforcement responsibilities. To address these additional aspects, DoT sought further recommendations from TRAI on July 31, 2018. TRAI responded by releasing a consultation paper in January 2020 , soliciting public input on the structure and functions of the MSB and the cataloging of reasonable TMPs. After extensive stakeholder discussions, TRAI submitted its final recommendations in January 2022. These included detailed guidelines on acceptable TMPs and proposed the formation of a multi-stakeholder advisory body to support the DoT in overseeing net neutrality compliance. Legality of Airtel’s actions (or lack thereof) The Unified License for Internet Service Providers is a license issued by the DoT that consolidates multiple telecom services, enabling providers to offer internet, telephony, and mobile services under a single framework. Clause 2.1 of this license guarantees subscribers “ unrestricted access to all content available on the Internet, except for content restricted by the Licensor or designated authority under law .” Building on this, the Regulatory Framework on Net Neutrality – based on recommendations from the TRAI – led to amendments in various internet service license agreements. These amendments were formalised by the DoT on September 26, 2018, as the Amendments to the Unified Licenses as per the Regulatory Framework , strengthening the licensing terms with Clause 2.3, which imposes a binding obligation on internet providers to treat all internet traffic non-discriminatorily, thereby embedding the core principles of net neutrality into the regulatory framework. It states: “ Principle of non-discriminatory treatment, definition of specialised services and reasonable traffic management and other exceptions: (i) A Licensee providing Internet Access Service shall not engage in any discriminatory treatment of content, including based on the sender or receiver. the protocols being used or the user equipment. (ii) The Licensee is prohibited from entering into any arrangement, agreement or contact, by whatever name called, with any person, natural or legal, that has the effect of discriminatory treatment of content. ” “Discriminatory treatment" has also been defined in the amendment [Clause 2.3 (iv) (b)] to include practices like blocking, degrading, slowing down or granting preferential speeds/treatment to any content. By embedding these principles into the licensing framework, the DoT ensured that net neutrality became an enforceable obligation, strengthening consumer protections and promoting a free and open internet in India. Additionally, the amendment Clause 2.3 (iii) (b) is an exception clause. Discriminatory treatment of internet content is generally prohibited, but it is allowed in limited and clearly defined cases under this exception clause. Such treatment is permissible when the Licensee adopts measures that are proportionate, temporary, and transparent, such as implementing reasonable traffic management, providing emergency services, or complying with legal orders from courts or government authorities. These exceptions ensure that while net neutrality is upheld, necessary flexibility is available for public safety, legal compliance, and efficient network management. Flowing from the above licensing conditions, the legal basis for blocking caused through the AI fraud detection system is not clear in law, as it does not emerge from any directions under Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000 or under a court order. Such a generalised blocking service at the level of the ISP constitutes an independent access restriction, and is beyond the actions permitted. Airtel’s plan to use AI filters may curb fraud and cyber-crime, but without strong legal safeguards and public oversight it invites abuse. An opaque system can favour some sites and block others, leading to hidden censorship, anti-competitive conduct, and suppression of lawful speech. These risks grow when no one knows the rules that guide the algorithm, there is no independent review, and users have no clear way to seek redress. In February 2025, TRAI amended its Telecom Commercial Communication Customer Preference Regulation (“TCCCPR”) to require AI-based detection of Unsolicited Commercial Communication (“UCC”) over telecom networks, allowing action against unregistered senders and repeated offenders — but only based on verified complaints and set thresholds. While Airtel has introduced AI tools to block malicious links and detect spam, including on apps like WhatsApp and Telegram, website blocking falls outside the scope of TCCCPR. Our concerns Airtel’s announcement of an AI-based security system raises deep concerns about user rights and access to the internet. Conflict with the Net Neutrality principle: At the heart of the controversy is the conflict with net neutrality, a principle that mandates equal treatment for all internet traffic, without discrimination or interference by service providers. Under Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000, only the Central or State Government has the authority to order content to be blocked, and even that power must be exercised following strict legal procedures. ISPs do not possess unilateral authority to restrict access to online content. Any deviation from equal treatment of data – such as discriminatory blocking or throttling – is permissible solely under narrowly defined exceptions, such as reasonable traffic management or other legitimate grounds outlined in the Regulatory Framework [para 3.2.1]. Moreover, the Unified License explicitly mandates unrestricted access to internet content, as discussed above. With the 2018 amendment vide DoT’s Regulatory Framework, the obligation to uphold net neutrality is a formal licensing condition. This means any attempt by an ISP to block or discriminate against content, not backed by legal directive, is not just improper, but a direct breach of its license agreement. Lack of redressal and transparency : Compounding this problem is the lack of user autonomy. Subscribers are not offered the option to disable the service or override the blocking mechanism. The erosion of user autonomy is further compounded by the absence of a robust and transparent grievance redressal mechanism. While Airtel has acknowledged that users can report incorrect blocks, it has failed to disclose any details about the process. There is no information on how complaints are evaluated, the timeframes for resolution, or the criteria for reinstating access. In effect, users are left without procedural safeguards or clarity on outcomes. This lack of due process is especially problematic given the opaque, algorithm-driven nature of the blocking system, which is susceptible to false positives particularly in contexts involving nuanced political, cultural, or linguistic content. Without public access to the blocklist or a formal appeal system, users have no way of knowing why access was denied or whether the decision can be contested. This creates a black-box environment where private entities, without oversight, can restrict fundamental digital rights. Threat to privacy: The AI tool scans all forms of digital communication – ranging from emails and SMS to OTT platforms and web traffic – raising legitimate concerns about mass surveillance. Users are given no insight into what data is being analysed, how long it is stored, or whether it is shared with third parties. The absence of public oversight or an independent regulatory mechanism amplifies these worries, as users have no way to verify the tool's behavior or challenge wrongful censorship. In response to MediaNama’s query, Airtel stated that the tool does not log or store any user-level data. Centralisation of power According to the latest data from the T RAI as of December 31, 2024, the Indian market continues to be highly concentrated, with around 95% of subscribers served by just four providers. Reliance Jio Infocomm dominated with a 50.43% share, followed by Bharti Airtel at 30.62% and Vodafone Idea at 13.37%. Bharat Sanchar Nigam Limited held just 3.74% of the market, while the remaining fraction is split among smaller regional players. This overwhelming concentration of market power among a few telecom giants underlines the lack of real competition in India’s broadband landscape. In such an oligopolistic environment, users face limited options. If any of these dominant players adopt restrictive or flawed content filtering systems, such as AI-based blocking tools, the consequences are far-reaching. With so much control consolidated in so few hands there is a heightened risk of overreach. This centralisation of control not only threatens digital freedom but also undermines the principles of a free and open internet. This development sets a troubling precedent. Once the infrastructure for AI-based content blocking is established, its use can easily extend beyond fraud prevention to include the suppression of speech and dissent. In the absence of clear legal safeguards, such systems risk becoming instruments of surveillance and control rather than tools for public safety. These concerns are not merely hypothetical. A growing number of instances show licensed internet service providers in India blocking specific types of content with little transparency or accountability. A comprehensive study by the Centre for Internet and Society, published in January 2020, highlighted this trend by exposing major inconsistencies across ISP-maintained website blocklists. The findings suggest that ISPs are either failing to comply with official blocking orders or are arbitrarily restricting access without any legal authorisation. Our representation to Airtel Airtel’s AI-based content filtering system, while presented as a safeguard against digital fraud, raises serious questions about transparency, user autonomy, privacy, and the future of net neutrality in India. In a market dominated by a few powerful ISPs, such unchecked technological interventions risk creating a deeply imbalanced digital ecosystem where corporate decisions determine what users can access online. This move threatens to erode fundamental digital rights and set a precedent for broader forms of censorship and surveillance. We have written a letter to the Vice Chairman and Managing Director of Airtel, Mr. Gopal Vittal, to urge the development of a more transparent and participatory framework. Users should be informed, empowered to make their own choices, and given the option to opt in or out of such filtering mechanisms. This approach would not only better align with constitutional values but also safeguard user rights through accountability, legal clarity, and respect for individual autonomy. We have also filed RTIs with DoT and TRAI requesting information on any proposals, meetings, or communications between them and Airtel regarding their AI-based security system, including its legality, impact on privacy and net neutrality, and any related public consultations or internal documents. Ensuring transparency and accountability in such initiatives is crucial to preserving a free and open internet for all Indians. We remain committed to monitoring these developments closely and advocating for the protection of digital rights. Important documents Letter to Bharti Airtel dated May 23, 2025 [ Link ] Copy of RTI with the Department of Telecommunications dated May 23, 2025 [ Link ] Copy of RTI with the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India dated May 23, 2025 [ Link ] IFF’s previous work on Net Neutrality [ Link ]

articles.title: When KYC Becomes a Barrier: Supreme Court’s Stand for Digital Inclusion
articles.summary: In its April 30, 2025 judgment, the Supreme Court recognised the exclusion caused by E-KYC systems and held that digital access is a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In a judgement dated April 30, 2025 delivered in the case of Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors. , W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors ., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 the Supreme Court of India observed that the right to digital access is an intrinsic component of the right to life and liberty. This ruling and its accompanying observations serve as a strong foundation for future petitions advocating for greater digital inclusion. Why should you care? Digital technology has undoubtedly made many aspects of life easier but when systems rely solely on it, without offering alternatives, they can end up excluding the very people they are meant to serve. For many citizens, especially persons with disabilities and those from marginalised communities, the push for digital KYC has become a barrier rather than a bridge. But exclusion is not limited to the vulnerable and marginalized alone. Even educated and otherwise eligible individuals can be denied opportunities due to technical glitches. People are being denied basic services because of things as simple as a fingerprint mismatch, a technical error, or not having access to a smartphone or stable internet. This is not just a technical issue—it is a human one. A system that does not account for the needs of all kinds of people, including persons with disabilities, is not an inclusive system. Our Constitution promises equality to every citizen under Article 14. When policies leave out entire sections of society, especially the most vulnerable, it becomes essential to call that out and seek accountability. The Supreme Court’s recent judgment is a powerful reminder that digital access is not just a policy goal—it is part of our fundamental rights, and it must be protected as such. Background On 30th April, the Supreme Court in the case of Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 issued 20 directions aimed at ensuring that e-KYC and other digital public services are made “universally accessible.” In doing so, the Court underscored that digital inclusion is not merely a matter of convenience, but a constitutional imperative and declared digital access to be an intrinsic component to Right to Life and Liberty. This decision stemmed from two petitions filed by acid attack survivors, led by Pragya Prasun, and Amar Jain, a visually impaired lawyer. The petitions highlighted the challenges they face in accessing digital services, especially those that rely on facial recognition or eye movement for verification. The petitioners described how they are routinely excluded from essential services, as digital KYC processes frequently require actions such as facial movements or blinking—tasks that are not possible for many individuals with facial disfigurements or visual impairments. Financial institutions are required by law to conduct KYC (Know Your Customer) procedures to verify the identity and other details of their clients to crack down on fraudulent transactions. Aadhaar-based e-KYC enables the identity and address of a customer to be verified electronically via Aadhaar authentication, making the entire process paperless. The customer provides their explicit consent (by providing biometrics or OTP) for the e-KYC service to instantly generate an electronic, non-repudiable proof of identity and proof of address. This case is just one example where the process of authentication of identity verification has been digitised and the people have been excluded from the process. This reality also extends to various other marginalized populations who are getting excluded on a daily basis due to biometric mismatches and other Aadhaar based identity systems, which have been made mandatory. In this context, the judgment of the Supreme Court underscores the importance of digital access in today’s world and provides a solid basis for future petitions seeking to advance digital inclusion. Digital Identity Exclusion: The Persistent Problem The problem of digital identity exclusion has two key dimensions: one stems from authentication failures, while the other arises from the risk of being entirely removed from official records. In recent years, Aadhaar-based identity systems have been increasingly implemented by both Union and State government agencies across India to authenticate individuals for access to a wide range of public services. These systems are now widely used in public offices, hospitals, and schools. However, their deployment often lacks sufficient oversight and safeguards, posing significant risks to citizens’ sensitive data and potentially excluding those who lack access to or familiarity with digital technology. The debate regarding exclusion from access to services is not unique to e-KYC and has been raised several times, especially with regard to Aadhaar-based and biometric-linked identity systems. The aim of these systems is to verify the identity and address of an individual electronically, making the entire process paperless. The process commences with the customer providing their explicit consent (by providing biometrics or OTP) for the e-KYC service to instantly generate an electronic, non-repudiable proof of identity and proof of address. The government asserts that the digitalisation aims to enhance transparency, efficiency and saves time while safeguarding the rights of citizens. However, this has not been the reality. In the Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG) report on the functioning of UIDAI, 2021, it was acknowledged that the need for biometric update could arise on account of authentication failures due to incorrect biometric capture or poor biometric quality captured at the time of enrollment. Excerpt from the CAG report highlighting the problem of authentication failures Excerpt from the CAG report with recommendations Major instances of exclusion caused by technology have primarily resulted from fundamental issues in the authentication process. Recently, thousands of teenage ration card holders in Tamil Nadu were at risk of losing access to subsidised food grains under the National Food Security Act (NFSA) as outdated Aadhaar biometrics recorded years ago have stalled their e-KYC verification. Most of the affected teenagers are school students aged 13 to 17 from economically disadvantaged families. Their Aadhaar biometrics were recorded five to six years ago when they were under the age of 10, and the system now fails to recognise their fingerprints, official sources said. Similarly, beneficiaries above the age of 70 are facing verification issues due to faded fingerprints. Additionally, the e-KYC process has contributed to the exclusion of ration cardholders, especially among migrants. These concerns are not new, but they take on renewed urgency in light of the recent mandate requiring e-KYC updates. Individuals who fail to update their information risk being excluded from benefits under the Public Distribution System (PDS). As a result, many states are struggling to manage the e-KYC update process, even as millions have already been excluded from the food security net. This exclusion stems from the government's continued reliance on outdated data, largely due to delays in conducting the national census as well. In recent years, numerous reports have also highlighted cases of workers being wrongly marked as deceased in the Management Information System (MIS) of the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005 (NREGA). In reality, these individuals are very much alive but remain unaware that they have been removed from the system, a situation affecting many workers who were previously listed on the MGNREGS rolls without their knowledge. On January 01, 2024, the Union Rural Development Ministry made the use of Aadhaar-Based Payment System (“ABPS”) mandatory for the payment of wages to rural workers under the NREGA, and announced that non-compliance will be met with penalties. Further, the NREGA Mobile Monitoring System (“NMMS”) App which was made mandatory for workers to mark their attendance last year, continues to be inaccessible and inaccurate, costing them their daily wage. It has been pointed out repeatedly that as ABPS was made mandatory, NREGA account deletions shot up. In the 2024 Winter Session of Parliament, Congress MP K.C. Venugopal asked a question stating that 10.43 crore job card deletions had taken place under the NREGA scheme in the last four years across the country and sought to know whether it was because of the introduction of the Aadhaar-based payment system that became mandatory on January 1, 2024. According to data provided by LibTech India, a consortium of academics and activists, between April and September 2024, 84.8 lakh workers’ names were deleted from the scheme. Last year, LibTech data showed that 8 crore people were removed from the MGNREGA registry during the financial years 2022-23 and 2023-24. The true tragedy of these deletions lies not only in the loss of livelihood for millions of rural workers but also in the systemic failure to uphold the core principles of NREGA, a program intended to serve as a safety net for the most vulnerable members of society. In response to these allegations, the Union Minster replied by stating that the union government has no role in the deletion of job cards under the NREGA scheme and its responsibility lies with the state governments. Although state governments execute the deletions at the ground level, the Union government shares equal responsibility for the crisis due to its policy decisions, outdated guidelines, and failure to address underlying systemic problems. The Internet Freedom Foundation (IFF) , along with several other organisations and campaigns—most notably Rethink Aadhaar—has consistently raised concerns and written to various government departments about the risks of exclusion and privacy violations arising from the mandatory Aadhaar linkage for access to State welfare schemes. At IFF’s annual event, Privacy Supreme 2024 , held on August 22, 2024, Nikhil Dey, Founding Member of the Mazdoor Kisan Shakti Sangathan, shed light on the ground realities of the Aadhaar project. Through firsthand accounts, he highlighted how individuals have been excluded from pension rolls and other social security schemes, underscoring the harmful consequences of so-called “voluntary mandatory” schemes, particularly for rural communities. Therefore, implementing technological interventions in social security and welfare delivery—without first addressing the deep digital divide and low levels of internet literacy in the country—is a policy decision that inevitably leads to exclusion and infringes on the fundamental rights of countless individuals. Digital Divide The judgment also draws attention to the deep-rooted digital divide in India. The Court observed that individuals in rural and remote areas face poor internet connectivity, limited digital literacy, and a lack of regional language content—barriers that prevent effective access to e-governance and welfare services. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 The India Inequality Report 2022 highlights that 70% of the population either lacks or has inadequate access to digital services. Flagship initiatives like BharatNet, intended to expand rural connectivity, have fallen short of their targets. Among the poorest 20% of households, a mere 2.7% have access to a computer, and only 8.9% have internet connectivity. The report further reveals that only 38% of Indian households are digitally literate, with internet usage in rural areas standing at just 31%, compared to 67% in urban areas. Complementing this, data from the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India shows stagnation in wireless broadband subscriber growth. The trend highlights that a significant portion of the population still lacks the digital infrastructure needed for processes like e-KYC. Graph showing the number of subscribers every 4 months from 2020 to January 2025 The graph depicting wireless broadband subscribers in India every quarter from January 2020 to January 2025 shows a steady rise from approximately 650 million in January 2020 to just under 900 million by January 2025. A sharp increase is visible in the first half of 2020, with subscriber numbers jumping by nearly 50 million between January and July 2020—likely reflecting the urgent shift to digital infrastructure during the early pandemic. However, post-2021, growth becomes sluggish and uneven. Notably, between mid-2023 and mid-2024, subscriber numbers plateaued around 880 to 900 million, showing no substantial growth for nearly a year. This stagnation is not merely a statistical anomaly—it signals a deeper systemic issue: the calcification of digital divide. As access to wireless internet becomes critical for accessing education, welfare schemes, and employment, the lack of continued growth in wireless subscription suggests that large sections of the population are unable to cross the digital threshold. Key Takeaways from the Judgment The judgment authored by Justice Mahadevan clearly holds that the right to digital access is an essential part of the right to life and liberty under Article 21 of the Constitution. In today’s digital age, access to services like education, healthcare, banking, and welfare depends heavily on digital platforms. Yet, persons with disabilities, rural communities, senior citizens, women, and linguistic minorities continue to face serious exclusion due to inaccessible design, lack of digital literacy, poor connectivity, and limited regional language support. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 The Court specifically addressed the barriers in digital KYC systems, noting that visual tasks like facial recognition, CAPTCHA, or aligning one’s face are impossible for persons with visual impairments or facial disfigurements. As a result, they are often denied basic services. The Court affirmed that such exclusion violates both constitutional rights and disability laws like the RPwD Act, 2016 and the UNCRPD, and called for digital platforms—government and private—to comply with accessibility standards like the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG). Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & WP(C) No. 49 of 2025 Importantly, the Court recognized the petitioners’ right to demand reasonable accommodations and emphasized that digital KYC guidelines must be revised with accessibility at their core. In doing so, it issued 20 specific directions to ensure digital KYC is accessible to persons with disabilities, especially those with visual impairments or facial/eye disfigurements due to acid attacks. Excerpt from the judgment in WP(C) No. 289 of 2024 & W P (C ) No. 49 of 2025 This judgment is more than a legal win—it is a powerful affirmation that digital systems must serve everyone, not just the tech-savvy or able-bodied. As the India Inequality Report 2022 shows, digital gaps are also gendered—only 31% of women own phones compared to 61% of men. In a country where one failed fingerprint scan can cost someone their ration or wage, the Court’s recognition of digital access as a fundamental right is a step toward real, inclusive justice—but the true challenge lies in implementation. Even before this landmark judgment, various Courts had recognized the problems caused by biometric authentication failures and digital exclusion. But this is the first time the Supreme Court has directly addressed the issue from the perspective of persons with disabilities. For instance, the Madhya Pradesh High Court, in a judgment dated March 25, 2025, held that a person’s identity cannot be determined solely by a machine and that biometric failure cannot be grounds to deny legal rights. Courts across the country are now speaking in one voice: alternatives to biometric authentication must be provided. Excerpt from the judgment in W P No. 8990 of 2020 and WP No. 6148 of 2020 Such alternative systems are not just legal safeguards—they are lifelines for people excluded due to fingerprint mismatches, poor internet access, disabilities, or lack of digital literacy. The right to access public services should not depend on one’s ability to operate a smartphone or align their face within a frame—it should depend only on their identity as a citizen. Even in Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India , (2019) 1 SCC 1, where the Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of the Aadhaar Act, it clearly recognized that exclusion from welfare benefits due to authentication failure is unacceptable. The Court noted that while the Aadhaar system aims to reduce leakages and ensure targeted delivery, it cannot come at the cost of denying benefits to those who genuinely qualify. It stressed that individuals who are unable to authenticate due to fingerprint mismatch, iris scan failure, or disability must be given alternate means to establish their identity. The judgment explicitly called for "suitable provisions" to prevent exclusion caused by technological limitations. Government needs to do more As the government intensifies the efforts in the digitisation of public services, it must also invest in strengthening offline support systems to ensure no one is excluded in the process. Common Service Centres (CSCs), set up under the Digital India initiative, are vital in bridging this gap. Located in rural and remote areas, CSCs enable citizens—especially those lacking internet access or digital literacy—to access essential services like ID registration, utility payments, and official certificates without having to travel to government offices. To truly make digital systems inclusive, CSCs must be equipped to provide alternative enrollment options and conduct outreach programs that build digital awareness, ensuring that the transition to a digital-first model leaves no one behind. This point becomes even more critical in the backdrop of the government's flagship scheme, the Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (“PMGDISHA”). Launched in 2017, PMGDISHA aimed to provide basic digital literacy to 60 million citizens across rural India within two years. Between 2019 and 2022, the Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (PMGDISHA) received consistent funding, with allocations of ₹400 crore in 2019–20, ₹300 crore in 2020–21, and ₹250 crore in both 2021–22 and 2022–23. However, from 2023–24 onwards, no funds have been allocated to the scheme, despite having not fully achieved its stated objectives. The discontinuation of such a vital programme, intended to empower the country's most digitally underserved communities, is deeply concerning. PMGDISHA played a key role in enabling rural citizens to access essential government, healthcare, and financial services online. Its absence leaves a significant gap in India’s push toward inclusive digital transformation. Another pressing issue the government must address is the lack of effective grievance redressal mechanisms within current systems. The Unique Identification Authority of India (“UIDAI”), set up a grievance redressal mechanism centrally to receive grievances through different channels which are highlighted below. Excerpt from the CAG report highlighting the Grievance Redressal Mechanism The CAG report highlighted a critical flaw in the existing grievance redressal mechanism: the inordinate delay in resolving complaints. One key issue is that grievances raised at the Regional Office level often fail to reach the attention of UIDAI headquarters, significantly undermining the system’s effectiveness. Additionally, the current grievance redressal process appears to be designed with the assumption that users have access to mobile phones—an assumption that excludes large sections of the population, particularly in rural and marginalized communities. This disconnect forces many citizens to run from pillar to post in search of resolution for issues that directly impact their access to essential services and rightful entitlements. What is urgently needed is a grievance redressal system underpinned by procedural safeguards, similar to those established in the Right to Information Act, 2005. Under RTI, there are clear timelines—such as a 30-day limit for the Public Information Officer to respond—followed by an appeals process that escalates to higher authorities, including the Central Information Commission. At every level, officials must provide a written, reasoned decision under their name, thereby ensuring transparency and accountability. In contrast, the current Aadhaar-linked grievance system lacks both responsiveness and accessibility. This not only erodes trust in public institutions but disproportionately harms the most vulnerable, who may not possess the digital access or bureaucratic literacy needed to navigate such systems. A rights-based, transparent, and inclusive grievance mechanism is not merely a technical fix—it is essential to safeguarding dignity, equity, and trust in public service delivery. Therefore, it is constitutionally imperative for the government to design systems that are not exclusively reliant on biometric or digital authentication. In light of the recent precedents, re-affirming digital access as part of the Right to Life and Liberty should compel policymakers to integrate inclusive alternatives into all digital public service systems—because a truly just and efficient welfare system does not punish citizens for the limitations of the very tools designed to help them. Important Documents: Pragya Prasun & Ors. vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 289 of 2024 along with Amar Jain vs. UOI & Ors., W.P.(C) No. 45 of 2025 ( Link ) Vinod Kumar Meena vs. Life Insurance Corporation of India, W.P. No. 8990 of 2020 ( Link )

articles.title: A RTI based investigation of Misinformation Control and Fact-Check Units
articles.summary: This post is the Part 1 of our investigation around fact check unites based on RTIs filed with states and Union Territories. The responses reveal distinct models of fact checking that give rise to concerns for free expression.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr State governments are setting up, or claiming to have constituted fact-check units to tackle online misinformation. Through Right to Information requests to all States and Union Territories starting in July, 2024 we sought details on their measures. The responses reveal concerns including — vague procedures, risk of political bias, and lack of independent oversight — raising the threat of arbitrary censorship and erosion of free speech. In this blogpost, which forms the first part of a three part series, we will analyse one of the most common responses we received – criminalisation of spreading misinformation and subsequent involvement of law enforcement agencies. Why should you care? The way misinformation is tackled directly affects everyone as more people rely on online platforms to keep updated. Here, both misinformation and the censorship of speech impacts our fundamental rights to receive information and in turn free speech. When governments and law enforcement agencies are given broad, unchecked powers to monitor and remove online content without clear safeguards, it opens the door to arbitrary censorship, political bias, and suppression of dissent. Hence, without strong protections, systems meant to fight misinformation can easily be misused to silence criticism, stifle public debate, and weaken democratic accountability. Protecting freedom of expression, ensuring transparency in governance, and demanding fair and independent mechanisms are essential not just for activists or journalists, but for every citizen who values an open and democratic society. Background After the brutal terrorist attack in Pahalgam on April 22, 2025 we have witnessed rampant misinformation spike online. Social media platforms have become battlegrounds where false narratives, doctored videos, and unverified claims are spreading at an alarming rate, often faster than official updates can keep up. Fake footage of military strikes, misattributed images of destruction, and fabricated government announcements are being widely circulated to inflame public sentiment, rally nationalist fervour, or discredit the opposing side. At the same time there has been illegal censorship which has been implemented in violation of any principles of natural justice. In this context, it becomes especially important to understand how governments are currently responding to misinformation. At this point many have stated the need for the government to fact check more proactively. However, an expansion of any censorial power which accompanies it has already been attempted and struck down as unconstitutional. A short refresher on it may be useful at this point. The Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Amendment Rules, 2023 (" IT Rules, 2023 ") empowered the Central Government to establish a Fact-Check Unit (“ FCU ”) with broad powers to evaluate online content and enforce compliance. The terms "fake," "false," or "misleading" information, along with the vague concept of the "business of the Central Government," granted the FCU extensive discretion in determining what constitutes misinformation about government affairs. On September 26, 2024, in a landmark ruling , the Bombay High Court struck down this provision, declaring it unconstitutional (read more here ). Justice Chandurkar (the third judge), in his judgement , disagreed with the Central Government's argument that the right to freedom of speech under Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution included a "right to the truth," which would require the State to ensure the dissemination of only accurate information. He found that the IT Rules, 2023 violated Article 19(2) by imposing restrictions beyond its permissible scope, arbitrarily distinguished between digital and print media (violating Articles 19(1)(g) and 14), and vested unchecked power in the government without clear standards, creating a chilling effect on free speech. Furthermore, the IT Rules, 2023, introduced substantive obligations that extended beyond the mandate of the Information Technology Act (“ IT Act ”), 2000, especially Sections 69A and 79, and lacked necessary safeguards against misuse. Despite this ruling, numerous state governments have moved ahead, setting up their own FCUs (or similar bodies) to purportedly address misinformation online. A RTI based study of state level FCUs To better understand these evolving state-level frameworks, we filed two sets of Right to Information (“ RTI ”) requests with the Home Departments of all states and Union Territories in July 2024, followed by two additional sets in January 2025. The first (generic) set of RTIs focused on measures adopted to monitor and control fake, false, and misleading information, while the second (specific) sought detailed information regarding the legal authority, structure, composition, powers, and standard operating procedures of any established FCUs or similar bodies. It is important to note that we were unable to file RTIs with Rajasthan due to procedural barriers, including the mandatory requirement of an Aadhaar ID and a Google account to access their online RTI portal. The responses were systematically reviewed and analysed to assess the operational frameworks and practices of each state. A clear pattern emerged: either there was the involvement of law enforcement agencies and the criminalisation of misinformation, or the constitution of FCUs, which may also lead to criminalisation, alongside states citing confidentiality and claiming exemption under the RTI Act. Kerala 09.01.2025 Link 15.01.2025 Link 18.07.2025 Returned 23.07.2024 Returned This is the first in a three-part series on how states are tackling online misinformation. This first part explores the role of law enforcement agencies in addressing misinformation, the second part will delve into the constitution of FCUs, and the third part will examine states that have claimed exemptions under the RTI Act. Reliance on Law Enforcement Agencies Across Indian states, law enforcement agencies are playing an increasingly central role in identifying and responding to online misinformation, often through the criminalisation of spreading false information under criminal laws and the IT Act, 2000. While this reflects a heightened concern over the harms of misleading information, the strategies adopted by states differ widely in terms of scope, intensity, legal grounding, and transparency. A review of state-level RTI responses reveals significant variation in how law enforcement agencies monitor, act upon, and regulate misinformation, highlighting the diverse approaches employed across the country. 1. Monitoring Mechanisms: Proactive Surveillance versus Complaint-Based Action Several states have established proactive mechanisms to monitor misinformation. In Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram , cyber crime units actively scan social media platforms. Similarly, Maharashtra and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands (Cyber Police) assign their cyber police teams to conduct real-time monitoring and intelligence gathering around potentially harmful or misleading content. Different districts in Jammu and Kashmir operate either Social Media Monitoring Cells, Electronic Surveillance Units (“ ESU ”), or Cyber Crime Cells, reflecting a more institutionalised surveillance structure. Conversely, in states like Karnataka and Tripura, misinformation monitoring is largely reactive. In a few districts of Karnataka ( Kempegowda , VV Puram , Shankarapura , CKA ) district police teams are tasked with this role. While in other districts ( Girinagar and KS Layout ), the aggrieved person may approach the police station and appropriate action under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (“ BNS ”) and IT Act, 2000 will be taken as per the law. If the news is disturbing public order then suo-moto action may be taken. In Tripura , while social media is monitored, some districts act only upon receipt of public complaints. Chandigarh (responses from DSP Cyber Crime to specific RTI, 2024 and DSP Cyber Crime to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) follows a similar approach, where complaints submitted to the Social Media Team may lead to action, but only after legal vetting and approval by competent authorities. 2. Takedown Practices and Legal Authority States vary considerably in their capacity and authority to act on content identified as harmful. Punjab (responses from Cyber Crime Cell to generic RTI, 2024 and Cyber Crime Cell to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) invokes Sections 69A and 79(3)(b) of the Information Technology Act, 2000, to initiate takedowns or legal proceedings. Maharashtra (Cyber Police) also invokes these provisions, in conjunction with the BNS, 2023, and coordinates with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (“ MeitY ”) and CERT-In for content removal under the Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 (“ IT Rules, 2021 ”). In Delhi , the police are designated as the nodal agency under Section 79(3)(b) of the IT Act, 2000, as outlined in the IT Rules, 2021. Content removal is overseen by the Joint Commissioner of Police. However, as with other states, there are no publicly available criteria outlining what constitutes "harmful" or "misleading" content, raising concerns about subjectivity in enforcement. Copy of Delhi Gazette dated December 26, 2024 The Andaman and Nicobar Islands represent a more extreme case. Here, the CID not only removes and prosecutes content under Section 79(3)(b), but they also resort to internet shutdowns in cases where misinformation is perceived to incite violence or disorder. While individuals may seek judicial redress if their content is removed, the discretionary power exercised by authorities remains largely unchecked. Law enforcement in these islands also collaborates closely with internet service providers, social media companies, and central cyber crime units for monitoring and enforcement. Screenshot of response from Superintendent of Police, CID, Andaman and Nicobar dated September 14, 2024 to the generic RTI filed in 2024 Chandigarh (responses from DSP Cyber Crime to specific RTI, 2024 and DSP Cyber Crime to generic and specific RTIs, 2025) requires written complaints before initiating takedown procedures, and only proceed once a competent authority grants permission. Interestingly, in Arunachal Pradesh , where 55 social media profiles were removed by the cyber crime unit via the Law Enforcement Online Request System (“ LEORS ”), authorities refused to disclose the rationale or case details, citing exemptions under Section 8(1)(h) of the RTI Act, 2005. This lack of transparency impairs public trust and impedes oversight. Similarly, one of the districts in Haryana – Faridabad claims to issue takedown notices for fake or misleading content but maintains no official records of these actions. Our Concerns The increasing reliance on law enforcement agencies and the criminalisation of misinformation raise significant concerns about the balance between safeguarding public order and protecting citizens' rights to free speech. While curbing harmful content is a legitimate concern, the lack of clear, transparent guidelines for identifying and managing misinformation leaves room for potential abuse of power. The broad discretion granted to law enforcement—whether at the district level in Karnataka or through Delhi Police—raises the risk of arbitrary enforcement. This could lead to selective action, where certain viewpoints or groups are disproportionately targeted, especially when definitions of "fake" or "misleading" content are vague and open to interpretation. States like Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram, where cyber crime units actively monitor social media, and Tripura, which relies on public complaints, exemplify how the lack of specific criteria can lead to biased or politically motivated action, suppressing dissenting voices or minority opinions. In more extreme cases, such as the internet shutdowns in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, law enforcement overreach in the name of combating misinformation disrupts citizens' access to information and stifles public discourse. This limits the free flow of ideas, further compounding risks to civil liberties. The threats posed by criminal prosecutions The responses to our RTIs also reflect the growing use of the BNS, 2023, as a formalised tool for criminalising digital speech. Provisions such as Section 353, which targets the circulation of false information likely to cause public alarm, and Section 197(1)(d), which addresses actions prejudicial to national integration, have been invoked by several states alongside the IT Act, 2000. These provisions prescribe significant penalties, underscoring the shift towards punitive state responses. The vague language in these laws—such as references to "public tranquillity" or "prejudice to national integrity"—further invites subjective interpretation and potential misuse. Section 69A of the IT Act, 2000, which empowers the government to block online content, also ties non-compliance with blocking orders to criminal penalties, including imprisonment of up to seven years. In this context, the Madras High Court's judgment in A. Kamala v. The State and Others offers a crucial counterpoint to the criminalisation trend. Responding to the detention of an individual under the Tamil Nadu Goondas Act, 1982, for allegedly circulating false information about a tender process, the Court asserted that not all instances of misinformation justify criminal prosecution—especially when there is no immediate threat to public order. The Court further underscored the need for preventive detention to be grounded in clear, tangible evidence, warning against the misuse of "public order" as a justification for suppressing dissent. The Court's emphasis on protecting social media expressions, even if critical or unpleasant, highlights the importance of safeguarding free speech in a climate where misinformation laws are applied inconsistently and arbitrarily. Unfortunately, governments across India are increasingly turning to laws like the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (“ UAPA ”), 1967, and sedition provisions to criminalise misinformation, particularly when it is critical of the state (see here and here ). These laws, originally designed to address genuine threats to national security, have been extended to target online speech, where the broad and often vague definitions of “unlawful activities” and “sedition” can be leveraged to suppress dissent. This trend is troubling, as it risks misusing these provisions to stifle legitimate political discourse, further eroding protections for free expression in the digital era. Conclusion The state-level responses to misinformation reveal key concerns around freedom of speech. The growing role of law enforcement and criminalisation of misinformation, combined with the absence of clear, standardised criteria, raises the risk of arbitrary enforcement and censorship. Without transparent guidelines, legitimate dissent may be wrongly targeted. The lack of uniformity across states—some using police, others cyber units or complaint-based systems—leads to inconsistent treatment of citizens based on geography. This fragmented approach heightens the risk of biased regulation and misuse of power. A more transparent, standardised, and legally grounded framework is urgently needed. Clear definitions, independent oversight, and safeguards must be put in place to ensure efforts to combat misinformation do not compromise democratic freedoms.

articles.title: How Ranveer Allahbadia’s Controversy on ‘India’s Got Latent’ could bring in more stringent OTT regulations
articles.summary: In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that are potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill. Background In early February 2025, video clips from comedian Samay Raina’s YouTube panel show India’s Got Latent — featuring Ranveer “BeerBiceps” Allahbadia, Ashish Chanchlani, Jaspreet Singh, and Apoorva Mukhija—went viral. Some viewers condemned the jokes, which used explicit sexual references to parents, as vulgar and obscene. The backlash centred on Raina and Allahbadia. On 10 February 2025 the Guwahati Police registered a first‑information report (“FIR”) against the five creators for “promoting obscenity”, citing: (a) Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (“BNS”) Sections 79, 95, 294, 296; (b) Cinematograph Act, 1952 Sections 4, 7; and the (c) Indecent Representation of Women (Prohibition) Act, 1986 Sections 4, 6. The Maharashtra Cyber Department followed suit, booking them under BNS Sections 79, 196, 296, 299 and s. 67 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 at Nodal Cyber Police Station, Thane. Press reports also point to an FIR in Jaipur . Confronted with parallel proceedings in three states, Allahbadia petitioned the Supreme Court (W.P. (Crl.) 85/2025) to quash—or at least consolidate—the Assam and Maharashtra cases. His lawyers mentioned the case before a Bench headed by the Chief Justice of India on 14 February for urgent listing, and the Court scheduled it for hearing on 18 February 2025. Timeline of court proceedings The legal roller‑coaster began on 14 February 2025, when Ranveer Allahbadia hurried to the Supreme Court seeking protection from arrest and a cascade of FIRs registered across the country over an episode of India’s Got Latent . On 18 February 2025, the Bench granted him interim relief but attached four tight conditions: he must cooperate fully with investigators, surrender his passport, remain in India, and halt all new episodes of his show. During the hearing Justice Vikram Nath stated the programme’s language “dirty” and “perverted.” Excerpt from the SC order dated 18.02.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 That same afternoon Ashish Chanchlani, another panellist, secured parallel interim protection from the Gauhati High Court; three days later, the Supreme Court formally tagged his case to Allahbadia’s petition. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 18.02.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 A semblance of reprieve arrived on 3 March. Acknowledging that the podcast supported roughly 280 jobs, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to restart production—provided he kept the conversation “decent” and steered clear of matters under judicial consideration. Crucially, the Bench asked the Solicitor General to draft proposals for a national regime to regulate online content without trampling free‑speech safeguards. This gave rise to fears of the revival of the Broadcasting Bill which IFF has commented on and analysed extensively. Excerpt from the SC order dated 03.03.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Procedural hearings followed. On 7 March the Gauhati High Court extended anticipatory bail to Chanchlani, while Allahbadia, on 1 April, filed an affidavit detailing his cooperation with the National Commission for Women and cyber‑police officials. The Solicitor General reported that investigations in Maharashtra and Assam were nearing completion, so the Court kept interim protections alive. The case soon intersected with disability rights. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 07.03.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 Excerpt from the SC order dated 01.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 On 21 April, the State of Maharashtra informed the Court that its investigation was complete and a charge sheet would be filed soon; Assam stated that its investigation was almost complete. Excerpt from the SC order dated 21.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Meanwhile, M/s Cure SMA Foundation sought to join the matter, objecting to Samay Raina’s allegedly ableist jokes. The Court agreed to consider the intervention and asked for a full petition, stressing that creative liberty cannot override the dignity of marginalised communities. Subsequently, matters escalated on the hearing on 28 April. While hearing Cure SMA Foundation’s petition, court observed that the issue of content regulation on OTT platforms falls within the domain of policy-making. The petition had raised concerns regarding the alleged circulation of vulgar and inappropriate material on streaming platforms. While the Court declined to frame regulations itself, citing the principle of separation of powers, it directed the petitioner to serve a copy of the petition to the Central Government for its consideration. On the same date of hearing, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to retrieve his passport but refused to club the Maharashtra and Assam FIRs on that date. Justice Surya Kant reasoned that the Assam complaint alleged distinct offences and its residents should not be forced to litigate in Mumbai. The Bench also chastised police for publicly insinuating Allahbadia’s guilt before filing a chargesheet. That day it instructed Cure SMA to obtain details from Mumbai’s Cyber Cell so every relevant respondents could served and be brought on record. Excerpt from the SC order dated 28.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Finally, on 5 May, the Supreme Court ordered the Mumbai Police Commissioner to serve notices on Samay Raina and four other creators, warning that coercive measures would follow any no‑show. The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from the SC order dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from Live Law reporting dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Proactive Move by MIB: Advisory to OTT Platforms on Content Regulation On 20 February 2025, two days after the Supreme Court lamented a “vacuum in law” on digital speech, the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (“MIB”) issued an advisory telling over‑the‑top (OTT) services to obey existing rules. Citing a spike in complaints from MPs, regulators and parents about “obscene, pornographic and vulgar” videos, the ministry reminded platforms that the IT Rules 2021 Code of Ethics, the Indecent Representation of Women Act, the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, the POCSO Act and the Information Technology Act 2000 all criminalise unlawful sexual content. It urged stricter age ratings and warned the two self‑regulatory bodies for OTTs to act against violations. While some may state that the advisory adds no new obligations; it merely re‑states laws that already bind streamers. Even so, the timing—just a week after a parliamentary panel decried “vulgar content” online—suggests the government is preparing the ground for tougher, possibly pre‑screening, controls on digital media. Excerpt from the advisory dated 19.02.2025 issued by MIB While the term "regulation" may sound like an innocuous word, it’s important to recall that the State’s previously proposed legal framework - Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill, 2023 (“Broadcasting Bill”), which was so draconian that it faced widespread criticism and was ultimately shelved. The MIB released the Broadcasting Bill 2023 for public consultation on November 10, 2023. The draft bill included “Over-the-Top” (“OTT”) content & digital news published by individuals under the regulatory ambit raising concerns for online free speech and journalistic freedom. Subsequently, in July 2024, another version of the Broadcasting Bill was released but never officially published by MIB, however, several unofficial copies of the 2024 Bill were circulated online.
