Squint Neon (“ Shaviya Sharma ”), where the plaintiff’s tweet about a “public political figure” triggered an outrage on social media, during which her personal and professional details including photographs and employer information were widely circulated. The Delhi High Court, while granting relief and directing X Corp. to release the Basic Subscriber Information of the offending accounts, held that the case did not constitute doxxing. It held that the information that had been shared by the accounts was not ‘private’ in the legal sense, as the plaintiff’s tweet included her initials and profile picture, making her easily identifiable to those familiar with her. Shaviya Sharma is one of the first cases in the Indian higher judiciary to discuss and adjudicate on a doxxing claim, and for that it is an important touchstone. However, its emphasis on the initial ‘anonymity’ of the information sets up a dangerous defence for future cases, and misunderstands the idea of doxxing. Doxxing is not just the revealing of personal information that is previously unknown. It is the sharing of someone’s personal information by a third party without their consent. The legal assumption in Shaviya Sharma is that once you have shared certain personal information with a third-party, you can no longer claim that information to be anonymous or ‘private’. However, this has been explicitly rejected by the Supreme Court in District Collector, Hyderabad v. Canara Bank, (2005) 1 SCC 496, where a two-judge bench of the Supreme Court held that privacy attaches to persons and not places, meaning that the reasonable expectation of privacy over personal information survives even if one chooses to share it with a third party. In the digital age, dangerous personal information such as one’s employer, contact details and likeness can be gathered through basic searches with minimal effort. However, the easy accessibility of that information does not necessarily render it ‘public’ information. Your photograph and name being displayed via a social media platform does not (a) take away one’s reasonable expectation of privacy over that information, and (b) does not give others a right to use that information without your consent. What to do if you are Doxxed? If you find yourself a victim of doxxing in India, it's crucial to take immediate and systematic action to protect your safety and seek redress. Here's a step-by-step guide: Document the Incident : Take screenshots of the offensive content, including URLs, timestamps, and any identifying information. This evidence is vital for both reporting and potential legal proceedings. Report to Platforms : Utilise the reporting mechanisms provided by social media platforms like Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), and Google to flag the content. These platforms are obligated under India's IT Rules to act on such complaints. File a Cybercrime Complaint : Visit the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal to file a complaint. Additionally, you can approach your local cybercrime cell or police station to lodge a First Information Report (FIR). If the local station is uncooperative, you have the right to file a Zero FIR, which mandates that the police record the complaint regardless of jurisdiction. Seek Legal Recourse : While India lacks a specific law against doxxing, various provisions under the BNS and the Information Technology Act, 2000, can be invoked. These include sections on defamation (Section 356 BNS), online harassment (Section 78 BNS), and breach of privacy (Section 66E of IT Act). Consulting with a legal professional can help determine the best course of action based on the specifics of your case. Conclusion Doxxing poses a multi-faceted challenge as an act of digital aggression. While some courts have dealt with doxxing manifesting as gendered online violence, instances of doxxing carried out against activists and journalists still remain outside the current legal framework. Recently, on November 8, 2024, journalist Rana Ayyub reported being doxxed when her personal contact information was shared online, leading to harassment and threats. Doxxing in this context is inherently political, intended to produce a chilling effect to stifle the speech of dissenting individuals. Significantly, in the face of rising instances of doxxing against activists, politicians and journalists, countries such as Germany and the Netherlands have adopted definitions that focus on the perpetrator’s intention to harm instead of the private/public nature of the information. These countries’ criminal codes penalise the public dissemination of any personal information of another person when intended to put that person or a person close to them in danger or to frighten/intimidate them. Legislative adoption of such a definition in India would move beyond the limits imposed by the Delhi High Court in Shaviya Sharma and would provide better, more universal protection to victims of doxxing. Important Documents Shaviya Sharma vs. Squint Neon & Ors., CS(OS) 134 of 2024 ( Link ) Information Technology Act, 2000 ( Link ) Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 ( Link )

articles.title: TRAI’s New Pilot Aims to Tackle Spam Calls Through Digital Consent Framework
articles.summary: In the first segment of the pilot project, banks and telecom service providers will work together to implement the digital consent framework, laying foundation for its sector-wise scaling to prevent spam calls. The post TRAI’s New Pilot Aims to Tackle Spam Calls Through Digital Consent Framework appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI) has launched a pilot project for the nationwide rollout of Digital Consent Management that would require telemarketing entities to take explicit digital consent from users, according to a press release . The authority will be coordinating with the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) for the rollout, and the banks and Telecom Service Providers (TSPs) will work together to implement it. This move would mandate financial institutions and TSPs to implement the framework to curb promotional spam calls that users receive. “Given the sensitivity of banking transactions and cases of financial fraud through spam calls, the banking sector has been prioritised for the first phase of implementation,” the Ministry of Communications (MCA) said in the press release. “This Pilot, running under a regulatory sandbox framework, will validate the operational, technical, and regulatory aspects of the enhanced Consent Registration Function (CRF) and lay the foundation for sector-wise scaling of the digital consent ecosystem.” Digital Consent Management To Prevent Spam Calls The regulatory body has observed that consumers made a large number of spam complaints against business entities that had earlier sold goods or services to them. These entities claim that they have taken the consent of the users for receiving commercial or promotional calls and text messages. Under the Telecom Commercial Communications Customer Preference Regulations (TCCCPR), 2018 , an entity can send promotional messages after recording consent with the Consent Registration Function (CRF). The entities also need to: Present the content of the consent acquisition template to the customer before taking consent. Take the agreement to the purpose of consent and details of the sender. Authenticate the customer by giving consent through OTP. Record revocation of consent by the customer via revoke request in a robust manner, which is immutable and non-repudiable, and as specified by relevant regulations. Record sufficient contact information, valid for at least 30 days, required to revoke consent and present it to the recipient to enable them to submit a request for revoking consent. TCCCPR, 2018, also allows an entity to send commercial messages to a customer irrespective of their Do Not Disturb (DND) preference if the entity has taken explicit consent from the consumer, the ministry said. “However, in many cases, these consents were collected through offline or unverifiable means, making it extremely difficult to ascertain their validity and genuineness. In several instances, consumers report that their mobile numbers have been acquired by the entities for this purpose through misrepresentation, deception, or unauthorised data-sharing practices,” said the press release. Background to the spam calls prevention measure In June 2023, the TRAI directed all access providers to develop a Digital Consent Acquisition (DCA) facility, a unified consent-seeking platform, which would allow service providers and principal entities like banks, business entities, and real estate companies to seek customer consent for promotional spam calls and messages. Additionally, later in September 2023, the authority requested entities such as banks, insurance companies, trading companies, and others that send commercial communications to consumers to begin onboarding the DCA system, as the initial deadline for onboarding the system was November 30, 2025. Why is TRAI mandating digital consent? The regulator believes that verifying consent for such spam calls and messages is a “formidable challenge” as the entities refer to the consent they took from consumers offline. Hence, to address this issue, TRAI made it mandatory for companies sending promotional communications to acquire consent digitally. It also requires mandatory registration in a secure and interoperable digital consent registry maintained by the TSPs for verification of consents. For the consent registration framework to function successfully, the authority believes that the onboarding of entities sending commercial communications is a “necessary requirement.” MeitY on an “Ideal” Consent Management System: In June 2025, MeitY unveiled a Business Requirement Document for a Consent Management System (CMS) under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023. This document lays down a set of guidelines for a Consent Management System (CMS) that would allow Data Principals, whose data is being collected, to decide whether they wish to grant or withdraw consent for data protection and processing through a single platform while complying with the DPDPA and its rules. The Act mentions a Consent Manager, which is a single point of contact between Data Principals and Data Fiduciaries, the entity that determines the purpose and means of processing personal data. To put it in simpler terms, the Consent Manager is a business that operates platforms to allow users to give, manage, review, and withdraw consent for processing their personal, identifiable data by companies and other commercial entities. The system begins the process of collecting consent when a data principal sends a request for registration or service onboarding that would require an entity to collect personal data for processing. Once users grant their valid consent, the CMS starts the process of validation. It verifies whether the data principal has granted explicit and lawful consent for a given purpose before a data fiduciary processes the data. Some unanswered questions: However, TRAI has not revealed further details about which banks are going to initially be part of this pilot project and how it will onboard these different financial institutions. The following question arises in this scenario: MediaNama has reached out to the State Bank of India, ICICI Bank, and HDFC Bank, seeking detailed answers to the following questions: How will the banks be onboarded for this pilot project? Is your bank going to be part of this pilot program? We will update the article with the responses from the aforementioned banks. Also read: US FTC Sues Uber For Charging Its Customers Without Consent Apple Fined $161 million in France for Discriminatory App Tracking Consent Rules How Much Does Parental Consent Verification Cost Under India’s DPDP Act? The post TRAI’s New Pilot Aims to Tackle Spam Calls Through Digital Consent Framework appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: TRAI Sets Price Ceiling for Broadband Tariffs for Public Data Offices to Boost PM WANI Scheme
articles.summary: TRAI fixes broadband price cap for PDOs under PM-WANI, allowing FTTH at no more than 2x retail rates. The post TRAI Sets Price Ceiling for Broadband Tariffs for Public Data Offices to Boost PM WANI Scheme appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: Telecom companies must offer all their retail fibre-to-the-home (FTTH) plans to Public Data Offices (PDOs) for bandwidth capacities of up to 200 Mbps under the PM-WANI scheme, according to the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India’s Telecommunication Tariff (71st Amendment) Order, 2025 . This tariff order aims to rationalise broadband tariffs for PDOs under the Prime Minister Wi-Fi Access Network Interface (PM-WANI) scheme, setting the rate at no more than twice the price of retail connections for bandwidth up to 200 Mbps. PDOs include small local businesses like kirana stores or tea stalls that offer Wi-Fi hotspots under the scheme. TRAI aims to safeguard the interests of these smaller PDOs through the tariff order, given that such businesses have limited revenue potential. The regulator notes that, based on data from one of the largest Public Data Office Aggregators (PDOAs)—who aggregate and manage the Wi-Fi hotspots operated by PDOs—the majority of PDOs are small-scale entities operating on broadband plans with 100 Mbps bandwidth. While PDOs currently offer capacities of up to 100 Mbps, TRAI believes that plans with 200 Mbps capacity would be sufficient to offer future flexibility and support the smooth growth of PDOs and the overall expansion of PM-WANI. Capacity beyond 200 Mbps is under forbearance, and the regulator does not currently see the need to set a specific tariff for bandwidth above this limit. Some context: The tariff orders follow TRAI’s first attempt to rationalise prices for PDO broadband plans in August last year . At the time, it had proposed that telecom companies should charge PDOs the same rates as they do for other retail broadband connections. The rationale behind the price rationalisation is that the high cost of backhaul connectivity is hindering the PM-WANI scheme from achieving its targets. Backhaul connectivity refers to the link between a telecom company’s core network infrastructure and the smaller, local networks that serve end users. The Department of Telecommunications (DoT) informed TRAI that, under the guise of commercial agreements, telcos often compel PDOs to use expensive Internet Leased Lines (ILLs) to connect with public Wi-Fi access points. These ILLs can cost 40–80 times more than a retail broadband connection. In January this year , TRAI released a revised draft of the tariff order, setting PDO broadband prices at no more than twice the rate of retail connections. This move faced strong resistance from telecom operators, who argued that the relevance of Wi-Fi is declining as mobile data becomes more widespread and competitively priced. They also pointed out that 45% of PM-WANI public Wi-Fi hotspots are located in Delhi, raising concerns about the scheme’s effectiveness in enhancing rural internet connectivity. Additionally, telcos cited competition concerns , claiming that regulating PDO tariffs could deter them from investing in FTTH infrastructure. TRAI’s rationale behind the new tariff order: TRAI argues that a tariff framework is necessary to protect the interests of small-scale PDOs. Addressing the competition concerns, the regulator highlighted that there are only 3 lakh registered PDOs, accounting for just 0.7% of all FTTH connections. TRAI emphasised the PM-WANI scheme’s broader economic, social, and financial benefits. The December 2020 proposal for PM-WANI noted that the scheme could: Improve user experience and quality of service Be particularly useful in rural areas Provide additional employment to small and micro entrepreneurs A 2022 research paper about PM WANI echoed these benefits. The regulator set the price for PDO tariffs higher than retail tariffs because it noted that the PDOs aggregated by one of the largest PDOAs under the scheme were using their broadband connections for both personal use and external use. It mentions that while it has set the ceiling for PDO broadband prices at twice the price of a retail FTTH connection, telcos can charge PDOs lower tariffs as well. TRAI acknowledges that as PM WANI grows, the scale of data consumption at PDOs will increase. At such a point it will be reasonable for the regulator to re-examine the price ceiling. To address this, TRAI will periodically review the number of active PDOs and their corresponding data usage patterns. Also read: TRAI Proposes Tariff Cap for PDOs: Double the Rate of Retail Broadband to Boost Public Wi-Fi Access Is PM-WANI Failing to Reach Rural India? Delhi’s Public Wi-Fi Dominance Sparks Concerns With 98% Mobile Coverage, Do We Still Need Public Wi-Fi? PM WANI Tariff Debate Heats Up The post TRAI Sets Price Ceiling for Broadband Tariffs for Public Data Offices to Boost PM WANI Scheme appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Transport Ministry Confirms API Access to Vehicle Data, Concerns About Data Sharing with Private Companies
articles.summary: Although the Bulk Data Sharing Policy was scrapped in 2020, government organisations still have API access to vehicle data , raising privacy concerns about third party apps like VehicleInfo using this data The post Transport Ministry Confirms API Access to Vehicle Data, Concerns About Data Sharing with Private Companies appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The Ministry of Road Transport and Highways confirmed that government organisations have API-based access to vehicle data. However, the Ministry ignored questions about data sharing agreements with private entities. The Ministry responded to questions sent by MediaNama under the Right to Information Act, 2005 (RTI). The Ministry recalled that it scrapped the Bulk Data Sharing Policy, which initially aimed to sell citizens’ data collected by the government and its departments and agencies. However, the RTI reply did not clarify questions about the updated list of private companies accessing data from the Parivahan and Sarathi platforms. These platforms handle vehicle registration and driver’s licenses, respectively. MediaNama also asked about current data-sharing agreements and revenue earned—if any—from selling vehicle data to private companies, including account aggregators and third-party vendors. We also enquired if there are any audit systems in place to track data misuse. These questions didn’t solicit any response. How Do Third-party Apps Have Access to Government Data? Despite the Ministry’s clarification that government organisations have API-based access to vehicular data, many third-party applications on the Google Play Store still offer the data to users for free. This raises doubts about whether these apps have legitimate access to government-owned data. For example, the app “Vehicleinfo – RTO Information” has over 50 million downloads and an average rating of 4.5 stars. The official Ministry’s NextGen mParivahan app has over 60 million downloads with an average rating of 3.7 stars. Vehicleinfo claims to be “an all-in-one app for all your vehicle info needs and RTO vehicle information app.” This unofficial app can fetch masked user data by entering the vehicle’s registration number. Almost every app offering such data services includes the same disclaimer, stating that it does not affiliate with any RTO authority in India. The apps claim vehicle owner details are publicly available on the Parivahan/mParivahan website. Their disclaimer states, “We work as an intermediary platform providing convenient access to this information while upholding strict standards of data security and privacy.” Similar disclaimers can be found in similar apps like BikeInfo , Vehicleinfo – RTO Information . Violation of User Privacy Nonetheless, the unofficial app’s claims of protecting user privacy remain unsubstantiated. Because, along with the masked user data, the app reveals insurance policy details, including the policy number and insurance company. Scammers can use this information to file fraudulent claims and access sensitive medical data. In another instance, miscreants grossly misused user information available on such platforms during the 2020 Delhi riots. The New Indian Express reported in 2020 that apps like “RTO Vehicle Owner Info” allowed users to enter vehicle details and obtain information such as the owner’s name, allegedly to target members of a particular community. The Background In 2011, the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) created the Vahan and Sarathi databases under its National e-Governance Policy and released funds to RTOs to digitise the data. Later, in 2012, the government passed the National Data Sharing and Accessibility Policy (NDSAP) to facilitate access to government-owned, shareable data and information in both human-readable and machine-readable forms. This has enabled the government to form the Open Government Data (OGD) platform, which has been publishing the data since 2012. The platform currently has 234,230 API(s) . APIs, or Application Programming Interfaces, are rules that allow software to communicate with each other and thereby transfer information. Did the Government Sell User Data? Yes, after a few months of passing the Bulk Data Sharing Policy by MoRTH, the government revealed the revenue it had earned after selling the user data. In March 2019, the MoRTH implemented a Bulk Data Sharing Policy to monetise the data. Under the policy, commercial organisations must pay Rs 3 crore for one year of access to the Vahan and Sarathi database. Educational institutions and researchers can access the data for Rs 5 lakh. Starting in fiscal year 2020-21, the fee will increase by 5% annually. After four months of issuing the policy, India’s Union Minister for Road Transport and Highways, Nitin Gadkari said the government earned Rs 65 crore by selling access to the Vahan and Sarathi databases to 87 private and 32 government entities. Additionally, another RTI reply to questions sent by the Internet Freedom Foundation (IFF) shows that, as of August 1, 2019, at least 142 entities purchased vehicle and license data from the Ministry. Why was the Bulk Data Sharing Policy Revoked? To begin with, the Ministry hadn’t done any public consultations before issuing the policy. MoRTH officials said the data will be provided in an anonymised format. The policy states that the ministry shares complete data with certain enforcement agencies, banks, automobile industries, finance companies, and insurance companies. This allows third parties to access personally identifiable information, undermining initial efforts to anonymise the data. Moreover, the policy itself admitted that there is a possibility of triangulation, which refers to combining different datasets that, on their own, reveal little about the sensitive user information. However, when viewed together and matched with other datasets, it can lead to individual identification and compromise user privacy. Eventually, almost a year later, in June 2020, the Ministry scrapped the policy after issuing the guidelines. The Ministry cited the misuse of personal information and privacy issues as the reasons for scrapping the policy. Some Unanswered Questions It’s been five years since the government scrapped its data-sharing policies. Additionally, as per the RTI’s reply, the government is likely only sharing the data with government organisations. This leads to concerns about potential illegal data scraping and unauthorised data access by third-party apps which raises the following questions: How do VehicleInfo and other apps on the App Store access the government’s data? How do these apps have data on a vehicle purchased in 2021, a year after the government scrapped the data-sharing policy? Did these apps purchase the data when the policies were in place? If so, why is the government reluctant, as Nitin Gadkari stated in Parliament, to ask companies to delete the data they bought under the previous policies? Are they still allowed to retain and process the data? Should these apps be allowed to retain and process government data after the policy change? These questions lead to the possibility of massive illegal data scraping, a punishable offence under Section 43 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 , which imposes penalties and compensation on individuals or entities that damage computer systems or extract data without authorisation. Also Read: Road Transport Ministry scraps policy which allowed it to sell vehicle registration and driving license data: Report Data for revenue: Revisiting the Draft India Data Accessibility and Use Policy They isn’t considering ordering private companies to delete vehicle registration, drivers’ license data they bought The post Transport Ministry Confirms API Access to Vehicle Data, Concerns About Data Sharing with Private Companies appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: What Social Media Creators Need to Know About J&K’s New Advisory and Legal Risks
articles.summary: Jammu & Kashmir's Doda district administration has warned social media creators to not pose as journalists to take part in unlawful actions. The post What Social Media Creators Need to Know About J&K’s New Advisory and Legal Risks appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: We missed this earlier: Jammu & Kashmir’s (J&K) Doda district administration cautioned YouTubers and social media creators posing as journalists against participating in “unauthorised and unlawful activities”. In an advisory dated June 11, 2025, Doda’s District Information Officer (DIO) noted several violations, like intrusion into public offices and disruption of public order by these individuals. MediaNama has corroborated the document with the J&K administration. The development was first reported by The Chenab Times. What are the ‘unlawful activities’? The administration accused creators of committing four specific violations and outlined the following penalties: Unauthorised entry: Entry into government offices or private properties for filming or creation without due permission would be considered criminal trespass. Dissemination of false or provocative content: Publishing unverified, defamatory, or communal content on social media could amount to penal action. Impersonation as journalists: Individuals posing as journalists without proper accreditation or authorisation would face legal consequences for impersonation and misrepresentation. Public disorder: Content inciting communal disharmony or undermining government authority in the ‘sensitive region’ of J&K would be dealt with strictly. What should creators do? Accordingly, social media content creators must refrain from entering government or private premises without authorisation, publishing defamatory or false content, or impersonating journalists without registration with a recognised media body. Further, they should adhere to platform community guidelines and Indian cyber laws and fully cooperate with law enforcement agencies in case of notice or inquiry. The administration outlined the following measures in case of violations of the above guidelines: Registration of FIRs under relevant sections of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860 * , Information Technology (IT) Act, 2000, J&K Public Safety Act, 1978, and Unlawful Activities Prevention Act (UAPA), 1967. Blocking and suspension of social media accounts under Section 69A of the IT Act. Preventive detention for habitual offenders of the J&K Public Safety Act. Seizure of electronic devices used in unlawful activities. Imposition of fines and imprisonment under legal provisions. Travel restrictions or cancellation of Overseas Citizenship of India (OCI) status in case of anti-national activity. Furthermore, the Press Council of India’s norms of journalistic conduct and the IT Rules, 2021 apply to digital creators as well. Background This advisory comes in the wake of the Indian Government instituting digital censorship after the terrorist attack in J&K’s Pahalgam on April 22, 2025. The action applied to social media profiles of Indian content creators, news websites, independent journalists, and Pakistani individuals as well. Alongside this, the Ministry of Information & Broadcasting (MIB) restricted live coverage of defence operations and movement of security forces by media outlets. Further, while the advisory sought compliance of digital and traditional media channels and social media users with “existing laws”, this generic mention could potentially conflate broadcast and digital regimes, claimed legal experts conversing with MediaNama. Why this matters This advisory highlights growing tensions between journalists and state regulation in conflict-sensitive regions like J&K. Furthermore, the J&K Govt invoking serious legal provisions under the UAPA and mandating preventive detention signals a hardline approach that could deter misinformation, but also risks curbing independent reportage. Alongside this, the move raises concerns around freedom of speech and expression, given the blurring of lines between content creation and journalism in the digital age. A similar sentiment was also echoed by JioStar’s Vice Chairman and former journalist Uday Shankar in a recent address centred around the evolution of journalism. Shankar contended that technology, including cell phone cameras, has turned everyone into journalists, with the internet and social media platforms becoming sources of news for journalists themselves. This direction could also be viewed as a reflection of broader trends in India’s regulatory landscape, where digital speech is increasingly monitored in the name of national security and public order. More recently, social media platform X (formerly Twitter) challenged the Indian Government’s usage of Section 79(3)(b) of the IT Act to set up a “parallel content-blocking process” in tandem with the Act’s Section 69A. Clampdown on creators Elsewhere, YouTubers and content creators in India have recently been subject to scrutiny. In February 2025, reports emerged of the Indian Government mulling measures for age-based classification of content and disclaimers for digital creators with over 50 lakh subscribers or followers. This proposal followed the eruption of a massive controversy against comedian Samay Raina, and social media content creators Ranveer Allahbadia and Apoorva Makhija after their appearance on Raina’s ‘India’s Got Latent’ web show. This followed multiple previous guidelines regulating influencers and digital creators. Also Read: Full List: Digital Censorship After Terrorist Attack in Kashmir MIB Bars Live Reporting of Defence Operations, But What of Digital Platforms? Uday Shankar On The Death And Rebirth(?) Of Journalism *Note: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) replaced the IPC on July 1, 2024 . The post What Social Media Creators Need to Know About J&K’s New Advisory and Legal Risks appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: RBI’s New Guidelines For Electronic Trading Platforms Exclude Offshore Entities: What This Means
articles.summary: The RBI has issued its Master Directions for Electronic Trading Platforms, however they don't mention offshore ETPs or entities operating abroad. The post RBI’s New Guidelines For Electronic Trading Platforms Exclude Offshore Entities: What This Means appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) issued the Master Direction for Electronic Trading Platforms (ETPs) on June 16, 2025. However, these directions exclude any mention of offshore ETPs or entities operating abroad by an operator incorporated outside India. This contrasts with the 2024 draft directions, which stipulated the criteria for authorisation and other responsibilities of offshore entities. For context, ETPs comprise any electronic system besides recognised stock exchanges that facilitate transactions in securities, market instruments, and derivatives. To operate as ETPs, entities should be incorporated in India, have a minimum net worth of Rs 5 crore, and maintain technological infrastructure with a “high degree of reliability, availability, scalability, and security” concerning their systems and data. Why this matters The directions build on the central bank’s intent to review and update the existing 2018 framework for ETPs. In February 2024, the RBI released its ‘Statement on Developmental and Regulatory Policies’, during which it noted the recent increase in the integration of onshore forex markets with offshore markets, developments within the technological landscape and growth in product diversity. In a different vein, in 2023, the authority expanded its ‘Alert list’ of unauthorised forex trading entities. This repository contains the names of entities not authorised to deal with forex under the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999, or to conduct forex transactions prohibited under the RBI’s ETP Directions, 2018. Consequently, the omission of offshore ETPs in the final directions leaves a regulatory gap despite earlier efforts to bring such entities under oversight. Other minor changes The 2025 master directions include certain additions to the responsibilities of ETPs based on stakeholders’ comments and feedback on the 2024 draft. Operating framework: The directions outline two primary aspects of the operating framework of ETPs—access and participation, and risk management. Within the risk management framework, ETP operators should now also establish pre-trade and post-trade controls to reduce erroneous transactions like off-market quotes, fat finger errors , among others by members. Additionally, ETP operators providing algorithmic trading systems should ensure that competent and skilled persons handle such systems. Preservation, access, and use of data: In case the operator’s authorisation is cancelled, it should share all data about activities on the platform with the RBI or any agency it specifies in a form prescribed by the central bank. Reporting requirements: Besides other requirements, the entity should furnish any report or information sought by the RBI within a specified format and timeframe. Also Read: RBI proposes draft directions for Electronic Trading Platforms RBI Expands Alert List with 19 New Unauthorized Forex Trading Entities RBI adds eight more entities to list of banned forex trading platforms The post RBI’s New Guidelines For Electronic Trading Platforms Exclude Offshore Entities: What This Means appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: How NPCI’s New UPI Changes Will Impact Transaction Speed and Fraud Prevention
articles.summary: The NPCI prescribed a reduction in the response time of UPI transactions, effective from June 16, to enhance the experience of customers. The post How NPCI’s New UPI Changes Will Impact Transaction Speed and Fraud Prevention appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) has prescribed a reduction in the response time of UPI transactions effective from June 16, 2025. As per a circular dated April 26, the entity has significantly brought down the response time of various UPI APIs to enhance customer experience. The new response times are as follows: Request and response pay APIs: From 30 seconds to 15 seconds. Check transaction status APIs: From 30 seconds to 10 seconds. Transaction reversal API: From 30 seconds to 10 seconds. Validate address (pay and collect): From 15 seconds to 10 seconds. The NPCI also directed payment service providers (PSPs) and other member banks to update necessary changes to accommodate the responses within the revised time limits. However, it cautioned against the changes impacting the technical decline (TD) threshold. Technical Decline arises from technical failures like connectivity issues, switch unavailability, and incorrect message data elements, among others. Other customer-led initiatives Besides these directions, the NPCI, in another circular, prescribed changes to its beneficiary name verification and display process during UPI transactions. With these changes, the corporation aims to boost customer confidence in money transfers to the correct beneficiary and avoid potential risks. Firstly, UPI applications should display only the ultimate beneficiary’s name to the user on the pre-transaction details page. Additionally, the NPCI prohibited the display of names extracted from QR codes, user-defined names of the payee, or any other logic within UPI apps. Secondly, UPI apps should disable any feature that allows users to change their beneficiary name for transaction purposes within the app interface. Members should adopt these initiatives by June 30, 2025. Why this matters These directions come in the wake of four outages in the UPI ecosystem from March to May 2025. While the NPCI initially attributed the outages to “ intermittent technical issues “, it later identified the excessive and repetitive use of check transaction status requests by PSP banks as the root cause. Accordingly, the corporation issued new directions to limit this API and other usage of other APIs. The outages are significant considering the high volumes amassed by UPI transactions and its generally high uptake. To explain, the UPI ecosystem recorded a transaction volume of 18.67 billion and a value of Rs 25.14 lakh crore in May 2025, according to NPCI’s statistics. This represented a 33% increase in volume and a 23% rise in transactions’ value from May 2024. Furthermore, as per the Reserve Bank of India’s (RBI) Annual Report 2024-25, UPI volumes surged 41.7% and transactions’ value increased 30.3% from the previous fiscal year. Notably, UPI accounted for 84% of the total transactions processed in India’s retail payment systems in FY25. Interestingly, a likely driver of UPI adoption among merchants could be the zero Merchant Discount Rate (MDR) on UPI and RuPay debit cards, unlike the 2% MDR on credit cards. However, with the rise in such transactions, fraud has also grown. For context, fraud in UPI transactions rose by 85% in FY 2023-24 in comparison to the previous fiscal year, as per data shared in the Lok Sabha. Accordingly, this could also be categorised as the reason behind trust deficits concerning UPI usage among the public. Background In a different vein, these developments accompany indications about the Government reinstating MDR on UPI transactions above a certain amount. However, the Finance Ministry has promptly dismissed these claims, terming them “baseless speculations”. Recently, the Union Cabinet also approved a Rs 1,500 crore incentive scheme for low-value person-to-merchant (P2M) UPI transactions. Also Read: NPCI Directs Banks to Show Balance After Every UPI Transaction, Among Other Guidelines UPI Crash Leaves Users Stranded: Is NPCI’s Monopoly a Weak Link? NPCI sends warning letter to fintech entities offering ‘unauthorised use of UPI API as a service The post How NPCI’s New UPI Changes Will Impact Transaction Speed and Fraud Prevention appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Zoomcar Confirms Cyberattack, 84 Lakh Users’ Personal Data Leaked
articles.summary: Zoomcar confirms cyberattack leaking personal data of 84 lakh users, including names and contact details. The post Zoomcar Confirms Cyberattack, 84 Lakh Users’ Personal Data Leaked appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: Zoomcar, the self-drive car rental firm, has disclosed in a regulatory filing that it suffered a cyberattack in which hackers gained access to the personal information of approximately 84 lakh users. The leaked data includes names, phone numbers, car registration numbers, personal addresses, and email addresses. The company stated that the incident occurred on June 9, 2025, and it became aware of the breach after bad actors emailed some of its employees, alleging unauthorized access to its data. Questions for Zoomcar: However, the company has not revealed any further details about the hackers or whether affected users have been informed. MediaNama has reached out to Zoomcar for more information. Has the company been able to identify the hackers? Have the hackers tried to reach out to the company seeking a ransom or threatening to make the data public? Did Zoomcar inform the affected users? Has the company been able to identify the vulnerability that the hackers exploited to gain access to the personal information of the users? If yes, has the company fixed it? We will update the copy once the company responds. Steps taken by Zoomcar: To enhance security, the Bengaluru-headquartered company has started implementing additional safeguards across its cloud infrastructure and internal network. It is also increasing system monitoring and reviewing access controls. “To date, the incident has not resulted in any material disruption to the Company’s operations,” the company noted in its regulatory filing. “At this time, there is no evidence that financial information, plaintext passwords, or other sensitive identifiers were compromised,” Zoomcar informed the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Why it matters? In recent times, bad actors have started targeting consumer-facing brands that provide services or sell goods. Hackers are now able to breach databases and access sensitive customer information with the help of emerging and sophisticated technologies. These bad actors then use this data to manipulate and scam Indian citizens online and via phone calls. In April 2025, the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched India’s first Digital Threat Report, 2024 , which highlighted how AI has enabled cybercriminals to exploit vulnerabilities in the cybersecurity systems of financial firms. The report noted that the average cost of a data breach globally reached an all-time high of $4.88 million in 2024, a 10% increase from 2023. In India, the cost was estimated at $2.18 million. The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) has warned that AI-driven cyberattacks will become one of the most scalable and adaptable threats by 2025, challenging traditional defenses and requiring innovative countermeasures. Protecting customer data is paramount, as a breach of data amounts to a breach of privacy. Companies must work with the government and cybersecurity firms to deploy robust safeguarding measures and build impenetrable firewalls to curb future data breaches. Similar incidents: Adidas recently experienced a cyberattack in which bad actors accessed “certain” customer data via a third-party customer service provider. The company clarified that it did not compromise passwords, credit card information, or other payment-related details. In May 2025, British retailer Marks & Spencer (M&S) suffered a ransomware attack that led to millions of pounds in revenue loss and temporarily halted its operations. Similarly, in November 2020, hackers gained access to the personal information of over 20 million BigBasket customers in an online attack . This included names, email IDs, password hashes, PINs, and contact information. The bad actors subsequently sold the data on the dark web for around Rs 29.5 lakh. The grocery delivery platform, however, claimed it did not store financial data and was confident that the compromised data did not include such details. In August 2020, RailYatri, a ticketing and travel website, suffered a data breach that compromised the personal details of around 700,000 users. The breach occurred due to the platform’s production server being exposed publicly for a few days. Also read: What Are The Risks Of UIDAI Sharing Non-Personal Anonymised Aadhaar Data? How OpenAI’s ‘Sign in With ChatGPT’ Could Expose User Data Across Apps, Raising Compliance Issues Google’s AI Mode E-Commerce: Data Privacy and Business Control at Risk The post Zoomcar Confirms Cyberattack, 84 Lakh Users’ Personal Data Leaked appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Why is content blocking and surveillance a challenge for IXPs? #NAMA
articles.summary: IXPs operate on a network layer that can't block or monitor specific content, experts tell TRAI. The post Why is content blocking and surveillance a challenge for IXPs? #NAMA appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: Internet exchange points (IXPs) have no ability to block a specific piece of content, the Executive Director of Extreme Labs , Raunak Maheshwari, stated during the Internet Society’s Peering RoadShow on June 6. The event focused on issues related to peering and interconnection in India. One of the key points of discussion was the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India’s (TRAI) recommendation that the government bring IXPs within the scope of network authorisation under the Telecommunication Act, 2023. One part of the recommended IXP authorisation is that IXPs must block websites/URLs and/or specific network subscribers as identified by the government in the interest of national security or public interest. “With respect to internet exchange, if I get an order saying block Google, I can shut down Google ports. That is all I can do. Nothing else. So if they come and tell me, ‘Please block the YouTube video which is showing some yoga poses not well done,’ I have no ability to do it,” Maheshwari explained. He gave the example of the government issuing blocking orders for specific posts on X (formerly Twitter), pointing out that only X would have the ability to block such content. He added that not even telecom companies can block specific HTTPS URLs. You can watch the full roundtable discussion here: So, who should carry out content blocking? Given that it is not feasible for IXPs or Internet Service Providers (ISPs) to block content, Maheshwari suggested that the government should send blocking orders directly to content delivery networks (CDNs). Similarly, Rahul Vatts, Chief Regulatory Officer at Bharti Airtel, mentioned that to simplify the process of content blocking, it makes sense to go directly to the source. “See, there are today, let’s say, 200 ISPs. All of them are not at the same level of infrastructure or feasibility of being able to set up infrastructure. For a large telco, very easy. Blocking order comes, the automated system punches it in, blocks it. But for a smaller player, it’s work. He’s not even sitting 24/7. And last few days, last month, there was a humongous activity happening at night. You know what was happening on the networks. So there were blocking orders coming like hundreds each night,” Vatts mentioned. Whether the government should go to the CDN for content blocking depends on the use case, Anurag Bhatia, a network researcher at Hurricane Electric, explained during the discussion. In cases where the government wants to block specific tweets or YouTube videos, it is feasible to go directly to the companies. However, there is another category of harmful content that the government seeks to block, which is located on low-traffic sites that aren’t really present on any CDN and are hosted outside India. “In that scenario, of course, they won’t be that easier to be blocked by just reaching to the source. Many of them would not really go to a large commercial CDN. These are like low-traffic websites. So in that scenario, of course, they can be blocked at the gateway level. They can be blocked by various ILD [international long distance] players or a bunch of ISPs who are essentially connecting India to the outside world. So once you cover that, along with the social media blocking, I think that probably covers 99.9% of the cases.,” Bhatia explained. Is the regulator conflating IXPs and ISPs? Bhatia mentioned that the blocking requirements could indicate that the regulator is conflating IXPs and ISPs, because there isn’t much to block at the IXP level. “At the IXP level, all they see are just packets flowing—frames flowing, essentially. They just see who is sending how much traffic. Beyond that, they can tap. If they badly want to, they can mirror ports and tap into things. But then it’s all mostly encrypted traffic,” he said. Today, a large number of over-the-top (OTT) platforms have encrypted their services. Similarly, many messaging platforms are end-to-end encrypted. Bhatia mentioned that even services that are not end-to-end encrypted are still encrypted using Transport Layer Security (TLS) . IXs cannot carry out surveillance or interception activities: To explain the challenges of interception and surveillance for IXs, Maheshwari compared IXs to the fibre that carries telecom data from one point to another, and to the common conduits through which fibre travels. “Now, would you say that because data is also going in this common conduit through the fiber, I would like to have the same regulations that are set up on layer 3 of the network, wherein actually you can listen to the data on the fiber conduits also? This is not possible, right? This is illogical. Similarly, internet exchanges operate on a layer, which is the framing layer, which is switching layer. They have no visibility into the packet header.” Maheshwari explained. For context, the Open Systems Interconnection model (OSI Model) includes seven layers that a computer system uses to communicate over a network. Maheshwari’s key argument was that the government should tailor regulations to what a network player can do at a specific level of the OSI stack. “The only security that you can put in is the MAC [Media Access Control] layer security. There is nothing else that can happen at the switching layer,” he emphasised. Impact of interception activity on telcos: Vatts pointed out that a large part of the data traffic today is video. “See, I watched YouTube or I watched content on Netflix. How is it relevant for a law enforcement agency?” he questioned. Vatts mentioned that the industry spent nearly half a billion dollars in the past year to store this data for law enforcement agencies. This is not the first time a telco has raised this argument during TRAI’s consultation on service authorisation under the Telecom Act. As part of its recommendations, TRAI suggested that the government allow telcos to share lawful interception systems (LIS) on a case-by-case basis. It also recommended that the government take up the issue of increasing interception costs with the concerned law enforcement agencies and explore mechanisms to suitably compensate service providers for fulfilling additional security-related requirements. While interception and surveillance requirements can be onerous for telcos, the security requirements at the ISP and IXP level tend to be different, because the former can identify end users while the latter cannot. “I have two connections at my home—one from Airtel, one from a provider indirectly connected to Extreme. I can challenge you—if Airtel wants, they can figure out and identify my connection. Raunak cannot. He technically won’t be able to,” Bhatia explained during the discussion. Also read: Should IXPs be licensed at all? #NAMA How Does Peering work? Key Peering Best practices for India: Executive Director of Extreme Labs, Raunak Maheshwari #NAMA Lowdown: TRAI Proposes IXP Authorisation, Leaves CDNs Unregulated The post Why is content blocking and surveillance a challenge for IXPs? #NAMA appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Ads Are Coming to WhatsApp Status: How Meta Will Use Your Data and What You Can Control
articles.summary: Meta will start displaying ads on WhatsApp status, which the company claims will help users find and talk to businesses easily about their products. The post Ads Are Coming to WhatsApp Status: How Meta Will Use Your Data and What You Can Control appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: Meta has announced that it will start adding advertisements to status on WhatsApp. This announcement comes as part of a series of new features for the WhatsApp Updates tab, including subscriptions for WhatsApp channels and channel promotion. Meta says that the inclusion of advertisements in Status will enable people to find businesses and easily initiate a conversation with them about the products or services they are promoting. The company explains that while users will begin seeing advertisements in WhatsApp status, users’ personal messages, calls, and statuses will stay end-to-end encrypted. This means that not even Meta can see the communication between two people. WhatsApp told MediaNama that the frequency of the ads varies based on how people use WhatsApp Status. “We’ll be listening to feedback and making sure the user experience remains high quality. If we need to, we’ll make adjustments,” the company mentioned. Some context: This service launch comes after the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) stayed the Competition Commission of India’s (CCI) directive prohibiting the Mark Zuckerberg-owned messaging app from sharing information with other Meta companies in January this year. In 2021, the company changed its privacy policy to allow cross-sharing of user data with other Meta entities. The policy did not allow users to opt out of this cross-platform data sharing. The CCI had found this policy to be in violation of the Competition Act and imposed a penalty of Rs. 213.14 crore along with a five-year restriction on WhatsApp from sharing user data collected on its platform with other Meta entities. While NCLAT has stayed the five-year restriction, other parts of the CCI order are yet to be decided, such as: WhatsApp must clearly explain the data it shares with Meta companies (including the purpose and use of said data). A restriction on using this data for purposes beyond its services in India. Providing users in India the option to opt out of non-WhatsApp data sharing through an in-app notification. Giving users the ability to review and adjust their data-sharing preferences within WhatsApp settings. In comments shared with MediaNama, the company says that it is confident that its plan is in line with legal requirements around the world. So, what data will Meta use to show ads? As per the company’s blog post announcing the development, the company will rely on information like the country the user is in, their city, the language, the channels the user follows, and how the user interacts with the ads they see, in order to show them the ads they would be interested in. “For people that have chosen to add WhatsApp to Accounts Center, we’ll also use your ad preferences and info from across your Meta accounts,” Meta says. Accounts Center is a place where users can collectively view their accounts across Meta services. This includes Facebook, WhatsApp, and Instagram. The company uses information from across those accounts to personalise advertisements for users. The Accounts Center allows users to change ad preferences for Facebook and Instagram. For Facebook , users have the option to manage information that Meta uses to show ads based on their user profile, and personalise ads based on their activity information gathered from advertising partners. For Instagram , users can select whether they want to see more or fewer ads about a specific topic (the same setting is also available for Facebook). What information does Accounts Center collect from WhatsApp? In an update to Meta’s privacy policy, the company now discusses details of the information that WhatsApp accounts linked to the Accounts Center share with Meta companies. This includes : Account information such as the user’s phone number and their WhatsApp profile photo. Age information, which helps Meta reconcile any age discrepancies and determine eligibility for Meta Company Products. The content of a user’s WhatsApp status, if they shared the same over on Facebook or Instagram. Device and connection information, such as the app version or your set device language in WhatsApp. Information related to how someone uses Channels, if applicable. WhatsApp collects information about user activity on Channels , this includes the types of content a user views and how they interact with it; metadata about Channels, Channel Content, and reactions by Followers, and Viewers; the Channels features a person uses and how they use them, in addition to time, frequency, and duration of user activities on Channels. This information allows the company to recommend Channels specific to a user’s country/local language, and provide additional Channel features. WhatsApp told MediaNama that adding WhatsApp to the Accounts Center is optional, off by default and can be removed at any point. Can users manage advertising settings for WhatsApp? As per an update that came soon after the ads’ launch on WhatsApp status, certain ad settings do not apply to users who have added WhatsApp to Accounts Center. This includes ad topics, categories used to reach the user, and audience-based advertising. For context, advertisers can choose to show their ads to certain audiences on Meta platforms like Facebook based on the audience’s activity and information. Elsewhere, users can manage some ad settings that are applicable to WhatsApp via the ‘Customize ads’ tab within the Accounts Center: such as viewing the advertisers they saw ads from, and the ads they have clicked on. Within WhatsApp, users can also hide or report an ad, and see information on why they are seeing a particular ad. Note: The post was updated at 4:39PM to include comments from WhatsApp. Also read: WhatsApp Introduces Private AI Processing, But Leaves Out Key Use Cases NCLAT Stays Competition Regulator’s Order to Halt User Data Transfer From WhatsApp to Meta MHA Report: Most Cybercrimes on WhatsApp, 43,797 Complaints Between January-March 2024 The post Ads Are Coming to WhatsApp Status: How Meta Will Use Your Data and What You Can Control appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: From Rhine to Ganges: Navigating Legal and Institutional Waters
articles.summary: There is an increasing policy engagement and advocacy emerging from various fronts on the possible European commitments and collaboration in the management of India’s river basins. One of the key learning experiences that is often suggested in this context is to reflect on Europe’s policy frameworks and institutional structures for improved outcomes from India’s river […] The post From Rhine to Ganges: Navigating Legal and Institutional Waters appeared first on CPR .
articles.content: There is an increasing policy engagement and advocacy emerging from various fronts on the possible European commitments and collaboration in the management of India’s river basins. One of the key learning experiences that is often suggested in this context is to reflect on Europe’s policy frameworks and institutional structures for improved outcomes from India’s river rejuvenation programmes. In India, the past decade witnessed an unprecedented focus and budgetary support by the Government of India (GoI) on river restoration, starting with the Namami Gange Programme (NGP) . India is contemplating expanding Namami Gange’s experiences into a policy ecosystem to revitalise its rivers. The Ministry of Jal Shakti (MoJS) recently commissioned a large-scale study on assessment and management plans for six river basins in India, namely Mahanadi, Narmada, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery, and Periyar, for improved river management along the lines of the NGP. In this context, how does the European experience offer insights to inform programmes like the NGP? In this commentary, we briefly outline some of the legal and institutional facets of the European experience and the lessons it holds for India. European Cooperation on Rivers The European experience of managing its river basins in the post-World War II period is often considered an exemplary case of regional cooperation in the management of freshwater ecosystems. This has resulted in the implementation of several legally binding directives, most notably the adoption of the European Water Framework Directive (EU WFD) in 2000 and Flood Directive (FD) in 2007. The EU WFD has proven to be a pivotal piece of legislation in Europe’s history , reflecting the Union’s commitment to cooperative transboundary water management. T hough the EU WFD appears to be a standalone legislative framework for water management, it reflects historical lessons learned from cooperation over important river basins — most notably the Rhine and the Danube river basins. Early Experiences Some of the earliest evidence of formal cooperation dates back to 1815 when European nations came to an agreement over navigation on the river Rhine towards the end of the Napoleonic wars. This culminated in the Final Act of the Congress of Vienna. Article 108 1 of the Final Act resulted in the constitution of the Central Commission for Navigation of Rhine (CCNR) . Subsequently, the Mainz Convention of 1831 and Mannheim Convention of 1868 accommodated various riparian concerns regarding Rhine navigation, but under the same spirit and principles embedded under Article 108. Cooperation over the Rhine became a template for other larger European river basins such as the Danube and Elbe. Moreover, the legal framework established to facilitate cooperation among the Rhine Basin countries manifested in the Rhine being the most used trade route in Europe for inland navigation underlying the economic prospect of cooperation over its waters. Institutional Adaptation: From Economic Rationality towards Managing Environmental Risks These early instances, however, created consequences affecting the aquatic health of the river — notably, increasing river pollution and the dwindling salmon population . With the conclusion of World War II, the situation worsened. The major European economies — coincidentally the Rhine Basin states — steadily shifted their focus on deteriorating water quality. The International Commission for the Protection of Rhine (ICPR) was established in 1950 through the cooperation of France, Germany, Luxembourg, The Netherlands, and Switzerland towards restoration of the river. However, only in 1963, 13 years after its inception, ICPR obtained legal personality 2 at the Berne Convention through Article 6.2 , which endows it with rights and obligations – allowing ICPR to act independently and being represented by its Chairman. The Presidency rotates among the Rhine basin states every three years. According to the Preamble of ICPR, the goal of the Convention is to increase multilateral cooperation to facilitate sustainable development of the Rhine ecosystem. Initially, ICPR’s scope was narrowly restricted to addressing water quality concerns. Later, it expanded its focus and undertook a broader role which fundamentally altered the pathways for Europe’s water management paradigm. Imprint of the Rhine Cooperation on EU’s River Basin Management Europe’s early experiences in managing large river basins like Rhine and Danube bolstered the development of important regional directives in the EU for environmental management – the most prominent being the EU WFD. Reflecting on the early European experiences illustrate that European member states developed adaptive management techniques from the Rhine experience that helped advance these regional directives. In this regard, three broad insights emerge: Learning from History : The success and frameworks established for the Rhine have significantly influenced the EU’s approach to water management. The collaborative approach to managing the Rhine has shown the benefits of transboundary cooperation , including improved water quality, restored ecosystems, and reduced flood risks. These successes have reinforced the EU’s political commitment to cooperative water management , shaping broader EU water policy. The early experiences with the Rhine navigation established interdependencies over time and manifested in the creation of transnational institutions, flourishing trade, and heightened consultation efforts. Setting Precedent : The Rhine cooperation efforts highlighted the need for preventive measures to protect water quality and ecosystems. Protection of Rhine water quality through the deployment of various economic and legal instruments, and its satisfactory implementation proved to be a guiding force behind EU’s environmental policy. Techno-legal instruments such as the Polluters Pay Principle , No Harm Rule , Precautionary Principle , Sustainable Development Principle , etc. emerged from the experiential wisdom accrued through the management of the Rhine and other important river basins. These environmental principles serve as the core aspects of EU environmental law and transboundary water management. For example, the allocation of costs based on the Polluters Pay Principle among the Rhine Basin States, as well as the industries located along the banks of the river contributed to the Rhine Action Programme . On a similar note, the No Harm Principle 3 was operationalised by ICPR as it played a pivotal role in the cooperation between the upstream and downstream Rhine basin states for pollution control measures. Institutionalisation of Cooperative Mechanisms: The River Basin Management paradigm in Europe was a gradual and incremental process. Insights offered by the functioning of the CCNR and ICPR have been central in shaping the approach taken by the EU’s management of its large river basins. For example, the River Basin Organisation constituted to pursue Integrated River Basin Management (IRBM) as mandated by the EU WFD is a culmination of various institutional practices that emerged from the functioning of ICPR and CCNR. In addition, the other single most important criterion for pursuing river basin management is to ensure coordination and cooperation across geographies and political actors. The Rhine experience greatly benefitted and advanced this cause. The international cooperation effort and its eventual institutionalisation for the Rhine restoration programme, have produced protocols for monitoring, data management, and coordination mechanisms.These protocols include consensus-based measures to maintain high-quality data standards – making it accurate and reliable. These efforts paved the way for the adoption of the EU WFD and played a vital role in achieving the WFD’s ambitious water quality and ecological objectives. India’s experience with NGP and what it can learn from the Rhine In the Indian context, the National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) has taken some important steps in rejuvenating the Ganga Basin. NGP, in its current avatar, aims to address diverse pollution sources by including the entire basin as a unit of governance. The Ganga River Basin Management Plan (GRBMP) , prepared by a consortium of seven IITs, is credited with its usefulness for planning in Ganga and remains foundational for the NGP. Like the Rhine experience, the Ganga rejuvenation started with modest and narrow efforts. It was only in 2016 that the effort received a significant legal fulcrum. Prior to 2016, NMCG had limited functional scope, restricting its role to funding specific projects for the Ganga Basin. It did not have a mandate to take cognizance of any threats to the Ganges or the power to issue directions to the concerned authorities/polluters. In 2016, under the Environment (Protection) Act of 1986, the role of NMCG was strengthened to enforce laws regarding pollution control in the Ganges Basin. NMCG is now a fully functional authority that is both a regulator and an implementer in pursuing the management of the Ganges Basin towards improving the ecology and aquatic health of the river. Besides pollution abatement measures, it is making a promising effort to improve river-city connection through the Urban River Management Plan (URMP) , standardizing data collection protocols, etc. In this context, NGP offers opportunities to establish itself as a template for India’s river rejuvenation goals. Way Forward More than anything, the Rhine and the larger European experience offers two critical lessons — a legal framework developed through a consensus-building approach and the constitution of a legal authority which should be supplemented by institutional and deliberative mechanisms. In this aspect, NMCG is still at a nascent stage. The formidable task for NMCG would be to address some of the complex areas of the river rejuvenation programme — such as inter-state and centre-state cooperation , that would be required for pollution management, especially its core aspects of infrastructure finance, and data sharing architecture. All this would require cooperation across scales and would be crucial in sustaining the programme post the mission life of the NGP which is slated to end in its current capacity in 2026. This blog is part of an ongoing project on Rejuvenating India’s Rivers, in collaboration with the National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG). Final Act of the Congress of Vienna 1815, Article 108 (CVIII) stipulates that when the states are separated or traversed by the same navigable river, the navigation related powers must be regulated by common consent. Legal personality gives an organisation its own legal standing in the court, i.e. ICPR is formally recognized as a legal entity and can be legally represented by its chairperson. No Harm Principle obligates parties to not have any adverse impacts on the other in a cross-jurisdictional setting and particularly directed to the upstream states . The post From Rhine to Ganges: Navigating Legal and Institutional Waters appeared first on CPR .

articles.title: We condemn terror – but blanket bans are not the answer
articles.summary: Read our statement on the advisory released by the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting on May 8, 2025, ordering intermediaries to remove all content originating in Pakistan.
articles.content: The Internet Freedom Foundation mourns the lives lost in the April 22, 2025, Pahalgam attack and affirms that national security is a paramount constitutional duty, yet insists that security measures themselves must remain within constitutional limits. The Ministry of Information and Broadcasting’s May 8, 2025, “advisory” ordering every OTT service, streaming platform and intermediary in India to remove all content “having its origins in Pakistan” exceeds those limits. Advisory on content originating from Pakistan In light of the Pahalgam terror attack, national security takes precedence. OTT platforms are hereby mandated to remove all content originating from Pakistan. #OperationSindoor #IndiaFightsPropaganda pic.twitter.com/fDBMWqLjBX — Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (@MIB_India) May 8, 2025

articles.title: Sustainable Mobility: The Urgent Need to Scale Battery Recycling for EV Growth
articles.summary: Introduction: India’s journey towards sustainable mobility is accelerating. Electric three-wheelers (E3Ws) are taking the lead, even in Dhampur, where they have become essential for both employment and local transportation. Conversations with e-rickshaw pullers, manufacturers, and battery dealers give crucial insights into the increasing use of e-rickshaws in small towns, the present battery recycling systems, and the urgent need […] The post Sustainable Mobility: The Urgent Need to Scale Battery Recycling for EV Growth appeared first on CPR .
articles.content: Introduction: India’s journey towards sustainable mobility is accelerating. Electric three-wheelers (E3Ws) are taking the lead, even in Dhampur, where they have become essential for both employment and local transportation. Conversations with e-rickshaw pullers, manufacturers, and battery dealers give crucial insights into the increasing use of e-rickshaws in small towns, the present battery recycling systems, and the urgent need to advance these efforts. This article briefly highlights these aspects. The E-Rickshaw Boom In the small town of Dhampur, Uttar Pradesh, the streets are buzzing with a new kind of energy. Electric rickshaws, often called mini metros, are now a noticeable sight. Katik Ahmed, a 40-year-old entrepreneur from the region embodies the local spirit of innovation. Coming from a humble background, Katik began his career in welding before venturing into the e -rickshaw business in 2010. His company, MK Rana Engineering Works, operates two manufacturing units, selling e-rickshaws for approximately Rs. 1,15,000 each. In the town, these are crucial for daily commute and local businesses, including waste collection by Gram Panchayats. He further informs that there are around 500 e-rickshaws in Dhampur and about 3000-4000 in district Bijnor. As per the data from the Parivahan portal (2024), Bijnor has a total of 3,380 registered e-rickshaws, with 3,355 being passenger vehicles and 25 being e-rickshaws with carts. This transition reflects broader national trends. E-rickshaws, three-wheeled low-speed vehicles powered by a lead-acid or lithium-ion battery have seen rapid growth in the states of Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and other regions, accounting for 91% of all e-three-wheelers (E3Ws) on the road (MoRTH 2023). Source: e-Vahan portal, Government of India, 2024 The figure shows a consid00erable increase in the adoption of electric vehicles across several categories in India between 2018 and 2023. The three-wheeler (3EWs) category has witnessed the highest adoption rate (52%) over the years. This comprises both rickshaws and autos. The percentage may be greater considering the high incidence of unregistered vehicles. The widespread adoption of e-rickshaws , even in small towns like Dhampur, is driven by the socio-economic benefits they provide. E-rickshaws are more cost-effective than ICE-based auto-rickshaws, with a lower initial price (Rs 0.6-1.1 lakh vs. Rs 1.5-3 lakh) and substantially lower operating costs (Rs 0.4/km vs. Rs 2.1-2.3/km) (Dasgupta, 2021). Electric three-wheelers are 13-46 percent cheaper than auto rickshaws, which are currently dependent on rising CNG, diesel, or petrol prices (Harikumar, Anand, Jain, & Phillip, 2021). E-rickshaws also provide drivers with significant improvement in their incomes. Take Ganga Ram Singh, a vegetable vendor in Dhampur who also drives an e-rickshaw. His vehicle has become a reliable source of income, earning him around Rs. 700-800 per day. Similarly, Chandra Prakash Chauhan, another driver found an economic alternative within a local community after being rendered jobless due to drug abuse. Factors such as increased daily trips due to reduced manual energy usage, increased carrying capacity (on average four-seater e-rickshaws), and standard per-trip fare further contribute to these financial gains, making e-rickshaws a more viable choice for unskilled individuals from low-income backgrounds. Besides, supportive government policies like the National Electric Mobility Mission (2013), National Urban Livelihood Mission (2013), Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana (2015), Smart City Mission (2015), Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles (FAME I and II) and several Production Linked Incentives (PLIs) have laid a strong foundation. Current State and Future Demands While speaking with Katik about electronic waste disposal, he mentioned that the dominant method is selling battery waste materials to battery dealers. An e-rickshaw requires four batteries, each costing approximately ₹8,000. After 15 months of consumption, operators sell discarded batteries to dealers for around ₹2,500. E-rickshaw operators generally charge their vehicles at home, where a 10-hour charge supplies sufficient power to cover approximately 80 kilometres. Battery dealers collect battery trash and provide it to 4-5 scrappers in Dhampur, each receiving around 20 batteries in a month. When interviewed, scrappers reported transferring collected waste, valued at ₹115-₹117 per battery, to registered recyclers in Joya (Amroha) and Bhojpur (Moradabad), with some preferring to send it to Rajasthan recyclers. These facilities sort, shred, smelt, and reuse valuable materials. Components like lead, acid, nickel, plastic, and cadmium are then utilised to manufacture new batteries and other products. However, as the number of electric vehicles on the road continues to rise, concerns will arise regarding the adequacy of the existing battery recycling infrastructure to handle this expansion. Electric vehicles are expected to account for 39% of total automotive sales by 2027, growing at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 68% over the next 5 years (Indus Law, 2022). Most of the growth is projected to come from the travel segment, in particular E3Ws and E2Ws. This is due to fixed duty cycles and companies (e-commerce, groceries, and shops) committing to go entirely green in the last mile of the delivery processes. The most recent Battery Recycling Rules, 2022, implemented by the Ministry of Environment, Forest, and Climate Change, aims to improve India’s battery recycling ecosystem. The regulations cover all types of batteries, regardless of their composition or intended use, and hold producers (manufacturers and importers) responsible for the collection and recycling of the batteries they introduce into the market. However, the existing literature points out several challenges: First, the guidelines lack clear criteria for evaluating and classifying used batteries that could still have a second life, such as those suitable for household or backup energy use. Second, there is no mention of labelling to aid in proper segregation and disposal. Third, the rules do not specify incentives for compliance or penalties for non-compliance. These gaps highlight that, although the current regulations are a positive start, additional improvements are necessary to ensure the success and sustainability of battery recycling in India. It is necessary to compile key data such as the proportion of market share between formal and informal recyclers, the methods employed by informal smelters, and the health risks faced by workers for refining policies. To enhance the recycling ecosystem, the government should prioritise closed-loop recycling, offer incentives for manufacturers to comply with recycling requirements, and work to increase overall recycling rates. Furthermore, promoting recycling-friendly designs and investments in advanced research and technology for better recycling methods will be essential for long-term progress. With the policy framework now established, it is equally important to focus on its effective implementation. A multi-stakeholder approach should be adopted to monitor and refine these rules as needed. Conclusion Dhampur’s adoption of electric rickshaws supported by an emerging recycling system, showcases commendable progress toward sustainable mobility. However, existing recycling efforts will be insufficient to manage the increasing battery waste. The faster we understand the challenges and solutions, the quicker we can achieve the goal of sustainable mobility. Addressing these gaps and accelerating recycling efforts are essential for developing a battery recycling ecosystem for the future. The post Sustainable Mobility: The Urgent Need to Scale Battery Recycling for EV Growth appeared first on CPR .
